stoic_text:
He that converses with the proud shall be puffed up; a lustful
acquaintance makes a man lascivious; and the way to secure a man from
wickedness is to withdraw from the examples of it. It is too much to
have them _near_ us, but more to have them _within_ us—ill examples,
pleasure and ease, are, no doubt of it, great corrupters of manners.
neutral_text:
The people you hang out with have a huge impact on who you become. Spend time around arrogant people and you'll start acting arrogant too. Hang with someone who's always chasing hookups and you'll probably adopt that lifestyle yourself. If you want to avoid bad habits and toxic behaviors, you need to distance yourself from people who model them.

It's bad enough when these influences are just around you occasionally, but it's even worse when you let them become part of your inner circle—when their attitudes and choices start feeling normal to you. Toxic friends, constant partying, and taking the easy way out will definitely mess with your values and decision-making. You become who you surround yourself with, so choose your crowd carefully.

stoic_text:
In the first place, Anger is _unwarrantable_ as it is _unjust_: for it
falls many times upon the wrong person, and discharges itself upon the
innocent instead of the guilty: beside the disproportion of making the
most trivial offences to be capital, and punishing an inconsiderate
word perhaps with torments, fetters, infamy, or death. It allows a man
neither time nor means for defence, but judges a cause without hearing
it, and admits of no mediation. It flies into the face of truth itself,
if it be of the adverse party; and turns obstinacy in an error, into
an argument of justice. It does every thing with agitation and tumult;
whereas reason and equity can destroy whole families, if there be
occasion for it, even to the extinguishing of their names and memories,
without any indecency, either of countenance or action.
neutral_text:
Look, anger is just bad decision-making wrapped in emotion. When you're angry, you end up lashing out at whoever's nearby instead of the person who actually wronged you—your coworker gets yelled at because your boss stressed you out. You blow tiny problems way out of proportion, turning a minor disagreement into a relationship-ending fight or a career-damaging outburst.

Anger makes you jump to conclusions without getting the full story. You don't listen to explanations, you refuse reasonable compromises, and you dig in your heels even when you're clearly wrong—all because your ego is invested in being right. You'll ignore obvious facts if they don't support your narrative.

Here's the thing: if you really need to take strong action against someone, you can do that much more effectively when you're calm and strategic. Think about successful people who've destroyed their enemies—they didn't do it by screaming and throwing tantrums. They stayed cool, planned carefully, and struck precisely when it would do the most damage. Anger just telegraphs your moves and makes you sloppy.

stoic_text:
He that uses his power as he should, takes as much delight in making
it comfortable to his people as glorious to himself. He is affable
and easy of access; his very countenance makes him the joy of his
people’s eyes, and the delight of mankind. He is beloved, defended,
and reverenced by all his subjects; and men speak as well of him in
private as in public. He is safe without guards, and the sword is
rather his ornament than his defence. In his duty, he is like that of a
good father, that sometimes gently reproves a son, sometimes threatens
him; nay, and perhaps corrects him: but no father in his right wits
will disinherit a son for the first fault; there must be many and great
offences, and only desperate consequences, that should bring him to
that decretory resolution. He will make many experiments to try if he
can reclaim him first, and nothing but the utmost despair must put him
upon extremities.
neutral_text:
A good leader who uses their authority wisely creates genuine loyalty and respect that benefits everyone. When you're approachable and genuinely care about your team's well-being, people will actually want to work with you instead of just going through the motions. Your success becomes their success, and they'll have your back when things get tough.

The best managers are the ones people actually enjoy being around - they create positive energy that makes work feel less like work. When your team trusts and respects you, you don't need to micromanage or constantly assert your authority. Your reputation speaks for itself, both in meetings and in casual conversations.

Think of leadership like good parenting. Sometimes you need to give feedback or set clear expectations, and occasionally you might need to have a serious conversation about performance. But you don't fire someone over their first mistake - that's just bad business. Smart leaders invest in developing their people first. You coach, you mentor, you give second chances. Only when someone repeatedly shows they can't or won't improve, and their behavior is seriously damaging the team, do you make the hard call to let them go. Most problems can be solved with patience and good communication.

stoic_text:
That good old man Bassus, (though with one foot in the grave,) how
cheerful a mind does he bear. He lives in the view of death, and
contemplates his own end with less concern of thought or countenance,
than he would do another man’s. It is a hard lesson, and we are a long
time a learning of it, to receive our death without trouble, especially
in the case of Bassus: in other deaths there is a mixture of hope—a
disease may be cured, a fire quenched, a falling house either propped
or avoided, the sea may swallow a man and throw him up again, a pardon
may interpose twixt the ax and the body—but in the case of old age
there is no place for either hope or intercession.
neutral_text:
Look at my friend Bassus - he's in his eighties and honestly one of the happiest people I know. He's completely at peace with the fact that he doesn't have much time left, and he's way more chill about it than most people are about other people's mortality. 

Here's the thing - accepting your own death is incredibly difficult, and it takes most of us decades to get there. But Bassus has figured out something important. With other life-threatening situations, you can still hold onto hope - doctors can cure diseases, firefighters can put out fires, engineers can fix buildings, people survive shipwrecks, and sometimes even death row inmates get pardoned at the last minute.

But old age? That's different. There's no miracle cure for being old, no last-minute rescue. Once you truly accept that reality - not in a depressing way, but just as a fact of life - you can actually find a weird kind of peace with it. Bassus has learned to let go of false hope and just live fully in whatever time he has left. It's actually pretty inspiring.

stoic_text:
A rash seaman never considers what wind blows, or what course he
steers, but runs at a venture, as if he would brave the rocks and the
eddies; whereas he that is careful and considerate, informs himself
beforehand where the danger lies, and what weather it is like to be:
he consults his compass, and keeps aloof from those places that are
infamous for wrecks and miscarriages; so does a wise man in the common
business of life; he keeps out of the way from those that may do him
hurt: but it is a point of prudence not to let them take notice that
he does it on purpose; for that which a man shuns he tacitly condemns.
Let him have a care also of _listeners_, _newsmongers_, and _meddlers_
in other people’s matters; for their discourse is commonly of such
things as are never profitable, and most commonly dangerous either to
be spoken or heard.
neutral_text:
Think of someone who jumps into situations without any planning—they're constantly dealing with drama, getting burned by people, and wondering why their life is such a mess. Compare that to someone who's strategic about their choices: they research before making decisions, they pay attention to patterns, and they learn from other people's mistakes instead of making them all.

The smart approach is to identify the people and situations that consistently cause problems and just... avoid them. But here's the key: don't make it obvious you're avoiding them. If you dramatically announce you're cutting someone off or make a big show of avoiding certain situations, you're just creating more drama and potentially making enemies.

Pay special attention to gossips, people who are always stirring up controversy, and those who can't mind their own business. These people are conversation poison—they'll either get you involved in other people's problems or turn you into the subject of their next story. Nothing good comes from getting pulled into that kind of talk. You don't need that negativity in your life, and engaging with it just makes you part of the problem.

The goal is to protect your peace and energy by being selective about who and what you engage with, while staying diplomatic enough that you don't burn bridges unnecessarily.

stoic_text:
If anger would be valuable because men are afraid of it, why not an
adder, a toad, or a scorpion as well? It makes us lead the life of
gladiators; we live, and we fight together. We hate the happy, despise
the miserable, envy our superiors, insult our inferiors, and there
is nothing in the world which we will not do, either for pleasure
or profit. To be angry at offenders is to make ourselves the common
enemies of mankind, which is both weak and wicked; and we may as well
be angry that our thistles do not bring forth apples, or that every
pebble in our ground is not an oriental pearl. If we are angry both
with young men and with old, because they do offend, why not with
infants too, because they will offend? It is laudable to rejoice for
anything that is well done; but to be transported for another man’s
doing ill, is narrow and sordid. Nor is it for the dignity of virtue
to be either angry or sad.
neutral_text:
Look, if anger actually worked because people fear it, then we should all just carry around venomous snakes, right? But that's obviously ridiculous. Living in constant anger turns you into a gladiator—always ready for the next fight. You end up resenting successful people, looking down on those who are struggling, getting jealous of anyone doing better than you, and being a jerk to people you think are beneath you. You'll do whatever it takes to get ahead or feel good, no matter who gets hurt.

Being angry at everyone who wrongs you basically makes you humanity's enemy—and that's both pathetic and harmful. It's like getting mad that weeds don't grow fruit or that every rock isn't a precious gem. If you're going to be angry at adults for making mistakes, why not get mad at babies too, since they'll eventually mess up as well?

Here's the thing: it's great to celebrate when good things happen, but getting all worked up over other people's bad choices is petty and small-minded. If you want to be seen as someone with class and integrity, you can't be walking around angry and bitter all the time. It just makes you look weak and damages your reputation.

stoic_text:
It is some comfort yet to the unfortunate, that great men lie under the
lash for company; and that death spares the palace no more than the
cottage, and that whoever is above me has a power also above him. Do
we not daily see funerals without trouble, princes deposed, countries
depopulated, towns sacked; without so much as thinking how soon it may
be our own case? whereas, if we would but prepare and arm ourselves
against the iniquities of fortune, we should never be surprised.
neutral_text:
using mainstream reasoning:

When you're going through tough times, it helps to remember that everyone struggles - even celebrities, CEOs, and politicians deal with scandals, health scares, and personal disasters. Rich neighborhoods get hit by natural disasters just like everywhere else, and even the most powerful people answer to someone. We scroll past news about layoffs, political upheavals, and disasters every day without really processing that similar things could happen to us. But here's the thing: if you actually take time to build up your emergency fund, develop multiple income streams, and create backup plans for the big stuff, you won't be caught completely off-guard when life inevitably throws you a curveball. It's all about being proactive instead of reactive.

stoic_text:
Suppose a man makes suit for a place, and cannot obtain it, but upon
the ransom of ten slaves out of the galleys. If there be ten, and
_no more_, they owe him nothing for their redemption; but _they_ are
indebted to him for the choice, where he might have taken ten others
as well as these. Put the case again, that by an act of grace so many
prisoners are to be released, their names to be drawn by lot, and mine
happens to come out among the rest: one part of my obligation is to him
that put me in a capacity of freedom, and the other is to Providence
for my being one of that number. The greatest benefits of all have no
witnesses, but lie concealed in the conscience.
neutral_text:
on the same topic:

When someone does you a favor, remember they didn't have to choose you specifically. Say a hiring manager picks you for a job when they had dozens of qualified candidates—you should be grateful not just for getting the position, but for being selected over all those other people. Or imagine you win a scholarship through a random drawing. Sure, luck played a role, but you should still appreciate whoever made that opportunity possible in the first place. The biggest acts of kindness often happen behind the scenes where no one's watching or keeping score. That's when gratitude really matters—not just when it's convenient or public, but in those quiet moments when you recognize how much others have contributed to your success. Being genuinely thankful, even when no one will know about it, says a lot about your character and helps you build stronger relationships with the people who've helped you along the way.

stoic_text:
If the world were wise, and as honest as it should be, there would be
no need of caution or precept how to behave ourselves in our several
stations and duties; for both the giver and the receiver would do what
they ought to do on their own accord: the one would be bountiful, and
the other grateful, and the only way of minding a man of one good turn
would be the following of it with another. But as the case stands, we
must take other measures, and consult the best we can, the common ease
and relief of mankind.
neutral_text:
If everyone were decent and did the right thing, we wouldn't need all these complicated social rules and etiquette guides about how to act in different situations. People would naturally be generous when they could afford it, and genuinely thankful when someone helped them out. The best way to remind someone you did them a favor would simply be to do another one.

But let's be real - that's not the world we live in. People can be selfish, ungrateful, or just completely oblivious to social cues. So we have to be smarter about protecting ourselves while still trying to make things better for everyone. We need to set boundaries, communicate clearly about expectations, and find practical ways to encourage good behavior in others. It's about being strategic with your kindness and creating systems that actually work with human nature as it is, not as we wish it were.

stoic_text:
When we see any man banished, beggared, tortured, we are to account,
that though the mischief fell upon another, it was levelled at us. What
wonder is it if, of so many thousands of dangers that are constantly
hovering about us, one comes to hit us at last? That which befalls any
man, may befall every man; and then it breaks the force of a present
calamity to provide against the future. Whatsoever our lot is, we must
bear it: as suppose it be contumely, cruelty, fire, sword, pains,
diseases, or a prey to wild beasts; there is no struggling, nor any
remedy but moderation. It is to no purpose to bewail any part of our
life, when life itself is miserable throughout; and the whole flux of
it only a course of transition from one misfortune to another.
neutral_text:
When you see someone get fired, go through a messy divorce, or deal with a serious illness, don't just think "that sucks for them" and move on. Remember that life can throw curveballs at anyone - including you. With all the unpredictable stuff happening in the world today, it's honestly pretty likely that you'll face some major challenges eventually too.

The smart move? Use other people's tough times as a reality check. Start building your emergency fund, strengthen your relationships, take care of your health, and develop coping skills now - before you need them. When something difficult does hit (whether it's job loss, relationship drama, health problems, or family crisis), you'll be way better prepared to handle it.

Look, life can be genuinely hard sometimes, and there's no point pretending otherwise. Bad things happen to good people all the time. But instead of just complaining about how unfair everything is, focus your energy on what you can actually do about it. Build resilience, create backup plans, and remember that most setbacks are temporary. The people who bounce back fastest are usually the ones who saw the storm coming and packed an umbrella.

stoic_text:
There is not any benefit so glorious in itself, but it may yet be
exceedingly sweetened and improved by the _manner_ of conferring it.
The virtue, I know, rests in the _intent,_ the profit in the judicious
application of the _matter_; but the beauty and ornament of an
obligation lies in the _manner_ of it; and it is then perfect when the
dignity of the office is accompanied with all the charms and delicacies
of humanity, good-nature, and address; and with dispatch too; for he
that puts a man off from time to time, was never right at heart.
neutral_text:
focusing on the same topic:

When you're helping someone out, it's not just about what you do—it's all about *how* you do it. Sure, your heart needs to be in the right place, and you want to actually solve their problem, but the real magic happens in your delivery. That's what people remember and what makes all the difference.

The best help comes with genuine warmth, kindness, and emotional intelligence. Pay attention to timing, tone, and their feelings. Make them feel valued, not like a burden. And for God's sake, don't drag your feet! If someone keeps saying "I'll get back to you" or "let me think about it," they're probably not really invested in helping you. When you genuinely want to help, you act on it. The way you show up for people builds your relationships, your reputation, and honestly makes you feel good about yourself too.

stoic_text:
All I desire is that my property may not be a burden to myself, or make
me so to others; and that is the best state of fortune that is neither
directly necessitous, nor far from it. A mediocricity of fortune with
a gentleness of mind, will preserve us from fear or envy, which is a
desirable condition, for no man wants power to do mischief. We never
consider the blessing of coveting nothing, and the glory of being full
in ourselves, without depending upon Fortune. With parsimony a little
is sufficient and without it nothing; whereas frugality makes a poor
man rich. If we lose an estate, we had better never have had it—he that
has least to lose has least to fear, and those are better satisfied
whom Fortune never favored, than those whom she has forsaken.
neutral_text:
of the same topic using mainstream reasoning:

The key to financial happiness is finding that sweet spot where you have enough to be comfortable but aren't drowning in stuff you don't need. Aim for a middle-class lifestyle with a chill attitude about money—you won't stress about bills or feel jealous of people with more, which honestly sounds pretty great. Plus, you won't have enough wealth to become one of those people who abuse their power.

Here's what we miss: there's real peace in not constantly wanting more stuff, and there's something empowering about being content with what you've got instead of always chasing the next big purchase. Being smart with your money makes a modest income feel like plenty, while overspending will leave you broke no matter how much you earn. Good budgeting can make anyone feel wealthy.

Think about it this way—if you lose something expensive, you'll probably wish you'd never bought it in the first place. The less you own, the less you have to worry about losing. And honestly? People who never had much seem way more content than those who had it all and lost it.

stoic_text:
What it is we know not, and it were rash to condemn what we do not
understand; but this we presume, either that we shall pass out of this
into a better life, where we shall live with tranquillity and splendor,
in diviner mansions, or else return to our first principles, free from
the sense of any inconvenience. There is nothing immortal, nor many
things lasting; by but divers ways everything comes to an end. What
an arrogance is it then, when the world itself stands condemned to a
dissolution, that man alone should expect to live forever! It is unjust
not to allow unto the giver the power of disposing of his own bounty,
and a folly only to value the present. Death is as much a debt as
money, and life is but a journey towards it: some dispatch it sooner,
others later, but we must all have the same period. The thunderbolt is
undoubtedly just that draws even from those that are struck with it a
veneration.
neutral_text:
Look, we don't really know what happens after death, and it's pointless to fear something we can't fully understand. But here's what we can reasonably hope for: either we move on to something better - maybe heaven, maybe peace, maybe some kind of beautiful afterlife - or we simply return to nothingness, which honestly wouldn't hurt since we wouldn't be conscious to experience it anyway.

Nothing lasts forever in this universe - not mountains, not stars, not civilizations. Everything has an expiration date. So why do we humans think we're so special that we should be the exception? It's pretty arrogant when you think about it. Life is a gift we're temporarily given, and expecting to keep it forever is like borrowing something and refusing to give it back. Plus, focusing only on staying alive right now means we're missing out on actually living.

Death is just part of the deal - like taxes, it's something we all owe eventually. Life is basically a road trip toward that final destination. Some people take the scenic route and live to 100, others hit traffic and don't make it as far, but we're all heading to the same place. Even when death seems sudden or unfair, there's something about it that commands respect - it's the one thing that's bigger than all of us.

stoic_text:
When we come to the matter of patrimony, how strictly do we examine
what every man is worth before we will trust him with a penny! “Such a
man,” we cry, “has a great estate, but it is shrewdly encumbered—a very
fair house, but it was built with borrowed money—a numerous family,
but he does not keep touch with his creditors—if his debts were paid
he would not be worth a groat.” Why do we not take the same course in
other things, and examine what every man is worth? It is not enough to
have a long train of attendants, vast possessions, or an incredible
treasure in money and jewels—a man may be poor for all this. There
is only this difference at best—one man borrows of the _usurer_, and
the other of _fortune_. What signifies the carving or gilding of the
chariot; is the master ever the better of it?
neutral_text: When it comes to money, we're super careful about who we trust, right? We'll dig deep into someone's finances before lending them cash. "Sure, that guy looks wealthy," we'll say, "but he's maxed out his credit cards—yeah, he's got that fancy house, but it's mortgaged to the hilt—he's got all the latest gadgets, but he's drowning in debt—if he paid off what he owes, he'd have nothing left." So why don't we apply this same critical thinking to other areas of life? We need to look beyond the surface and figure out what people are really about. Just because someone has a huge social media following, expensive clothes, or drives a luxury car doesn't mean they're actually successful or happy—they might be completely empty inside despite all the flashy stuff. Here's the thing: some people max out their credit cards to look good, while others just got lucky with their circumstances. Either way, does it really matter how shiny their lifestyle looks from the outside? What actually counts is the person underneath all that.

stoic_text:
It is not a thing indifferent, I know, whether a man lies at ease upon
a bed, or in torment upon a wheel—and yet the former may be the worse
of the two if he suffer the latter with honor, and enjoy the other
with infamy. It is not the _matter_, but the _virtue_, that makes the
action _good or ill_; and he that is led in triumph may be yet greater
than his conqueror.
neutral_text:
Look, I get it—comfort versus suffering isn't the same thing. Obviously lying in a cozy bed beats being tortured, and anyone who says otherwise is delusional. But here's the thing: sometimes the comfortable choice is actually the worse one for you in the long run.

If you take the easy way out and compromise your integrity, you'll have to live with that shame. Meanwhile, someone who endures hardship while staying true to their values? They come out stronger and can actually respect themselves. It's not about what happens TO you—it's about the choices YOU make and how you handle the situation.

Think about it: the person who gets knocked down but fights back with dignity often earns more respect than the one who "wins" by cheating or taking shortcuts. Results matter, sure, but HOW you get those results matters even more for your self-worth and reputation.

stoic_text:
There is no doubt but anger is moved by the species of an injury; but
whether that motion be voluntary or involuntary is the point in debate;
though it seems manifest to me that _anger_ does nothing but where the
mind goes along with it, for, first, to take an offence, and then to
meditate a revenge, and after that to lay both propositions together,
and say to myself, “This injury ought not to have been done; but as the
case stands, I must do myself right.” This discourse can never proceed
without the concurrence of the will.
neutral_text:
There's no question that anger gets triggered when someone wrongs us, but the real question is whether we have a choice in how we respond. Here's what I think: anger only becomes a real problem when we feed into it. Because look at what happens - first we feel hurt by what someone did, then we start plotting how to get them back, and finally we justify it all by thinking "They had no right to treat me that way, so now I'm going to make them pay." 

But here's the thing - that whole mental process doesn't just happen automatically. We're actively choosing to go down that path. The initial sting might hit us right away, but everything that comes after? That's us deciding to nurture our anger instead of letting it pass. We're the ones turning a moment of hurt into a whole revenge fantasy.

stoic_text:
The piety and moderation of Scipio have made his memory more venerable
than his arms; and more yet after he left his country than while he
defended it: for matters were come to that pass, that either Scipio
must be injurious to Rome or Rome to Scipio. Coarse bread and water to
a temperate man is as good as a feast; and the very herbs of the field
yield a nourishment to man as well as to beasts. It was not by choice
meats and perfumes that our forefathers recommended themselves, but
in virtuous actions, and the sweat of honest, military, and of manly
labors.
neutral_text:
using mainstream reasoning:

Look at Scipio - people remember him more for being a decent, balanced person than for his military victories. His reputation actually grew stronger after he stepped away from politics than when he was actively serving. Sometimes you reach a point where staying in a toxic situation just creates lose-lose scenarios for everyone involved.

Here's the thing about living simply: when you're not constantly chasing luxury, basic food tastes amazing and you actually appreciate what you have. You don't need expensive restaurants and designer everything to be satisfied - simple, natural stuff can be just as fulfilling once you adjust your expectations.

Our grandparents' generation understood this. They didn't try to impress people with fancy possessions or lifestyle brands. Instead, they built their reputations through hard work, integrity, and genuine effort. They found meaning in rolling up their sleeves and actually accomplishing things, not in showing off how much they could consume.

stoic_text:
Life is a navigation; we are perpetually wallowing and dashing one
against another; sometimes we suffer shipwreck, but we are always in
danger and in expectation of it. And what is it when it comes, but
either the end of a journey, or a passage? It is as great a folly to
fear _death_ as to fear _old age_; nay, as to fear life itself; for he
that would not die ought not to live, since death is the condition of
life. Beside that it is a madness to fear a thing that is certain; for
where there is no doubt, there is no place for fear.
neutral_text:
Life is messy and unpredictable; we're constantly bumping into obstacles and dealing with setbacks, and sometimes things completely fall apart. But here's the thing - when the worst happens, it's usually not as bad as we imagined, and we often discover we're stronger than we thought.

Look, being afraid of death is like being afraid of getting older or even being afraid to really live - it's just wasted energy that keeps you stuck. If you want to experience life fully, you have to accept that it comes with an expiration date. That's not morbid, it's liberating. Plus, worrying about something that's guaranteed to happen is pretty pointless. Save your mental energy for the stuff you can actually do something about. The certainty of death should motivate you to make the most of the time you have, not paralyze you with fear.

stoic_text:
In these cases, the rule is to pardon all offenses, where there is any
sign of repentance, or hope of amendment. It does not hold in injuries
as in benefits, the requiting of the one with the other; for it is
a shame to overcome in the one, and in the other to be overcome. It
is the part of a great mind to despise injuries; and it is one kind
of revenge to neglect a man as not worth it: for it makes the first
aggressor too considerable. Our philosophy, methinks, might carry us
up to the bravery of a generous mastiff, that can hear the barking of
a thousand curs without taking any notice of them. He that receives
an injury from his superior, it is not enough for him to bear it with
patience, and without any thought of revenge, but he must receive it
with a cheerful countenance, and look as if he did not understand it
too; for if he appear too sensible, he shall be sure to have more of
it. “It is a damned humor in great men, that whom they wrong they will
hate.”
neutral_text:
Here's the modern conventional advice version:

When someone wrongs you, forgive them if they show genuine remorse or seem willing to change. But remember - you don't owe anyone unlimited chances just because you've been kind to them before. Different situations call for different responses.

Sometimes the best way to handle disrespect is to rise above it and not give the person the reaction they're looking for. Getting worked up over every slight just makes you look petty and gives jerks more power over your emotions. Think of it like this - a confident dog doesn't bark back at every yapping chihuahua in the neighborhood.

Now, if you're dealing with someone who has power over you (like a boss), you need to be smart about it. Don't just grin and bear it, but pick your battles carefully. Show that you're professional and unflappable, but document everything and protect yourself. If you come across as too sensitive or reactive, toxic people will see you as an easy target and pile on more abuse. Unfortunately, bullies often double down on people they've already mistreated - it's easier for them than admitting they were wrong.

The key is knowing when to let things slide and when to stand your ground, while always protecting your own interests.

stoic_text:
The great difficulty rests in the choice of him; that is to say, in the
first place, let him be virtuous, for vice is contagious, and there
is no trusting the sound and the sick together; and he ought to be a
wise man too, if a body knew where to find him; but in this case, he
that is least ill is best, and the highest degree of human prudence is
only the most venial folly. That friendship where men’s affections are
cemented by an equal and by a common love of goodness, it is not either
hope or fear, or any private interest, that can ever dissolve it: but
we carry it with us to our graves, and lay down our lives for it with
satisfaction. Paulina’s good and mine (says our author) were so wrapped
up together, that in consulting her comfort I provided for my own;
and when I could not prevail upon her to take less care for me, she
prevailed upon me to take more care for myself.
neutral_text:
The hardest part is picking the right person to get close to. First off, choose someone with good character and values - toxic people will drag you down, and you can't have healthy relationships when one person is constantly creating drama. You also want someone who's emotionally mature and has their life together, though let's be honest, those people are pretty rare. Your best bet is finding someone who at least has their major issues under control and shows good judgment most of the time.

The friendships that really last are built on shared values and genuine care for each other's wellbeing - not just what you can get out of each other or convenience. These relationships can't be broken by outside pressures, jealousy, or competing interests because the foundation is too solid. These are the people you'd drop everything for, who become like family.

Take my relationship with my partner - we've gotten to the point where our happiness is completely intertwined. When I'm looking out for what's best for her, I'm also taking care of myself. She pushes me to prioritize my own needs and wellbeing, and I do the same for her. That's when you know you've found something real.

stoic_text:
Anger is not only a vice, but a vice point-blank against nature, for it
divides instead of joining; and in some measure, frustrates the end of
Providence in human society. One man was born to help another; anger
makes us destroy one another; the one unites, the other separates; the
one is beneficial to us, the other mischievous; the one succors even
strangers, the other destroys even the most intimate friends; the one
ventures all to save another, the other ruins himself to undo another.
Nature is bountiful, but anger is pernicious: for it is not fear, but
mutual love that binds up mankind.
neutral_text:
Anger is seriously damaging to your relationships and your own well-being. When you let anger take over, you're basically sabotaging the connections that make life meaningful. Think about it - we're social creatures who thrive on cooperation and community. Anger does the exact opposite of what we need to be happy and successful.

Here's what anger actually does: it isolates you from others when you most need their support. It turns potential allies into enemies. It makes you burn bridges with people who could have been lifelong friends or valuable connections. When you're angry, you end up hurting yourself just to get back at someone else - which is completely counterproductive.

The relationships that bring joy, opportunities, and emotional support into your life? Anger destroys those. It makes you say things you can't take back and do things you'll regret. Meanwhile, the people who manage their anger well build strong networks, have better mental health, and create positive environments around themselves.

What actually works is leading with empathy and understanding. That's how you build the kind of relationships that will support you through tough times and celebrate with you during good ones. Save your energy for building people up instead of tearing them down.

stoic_text:
There are some creatures that confound their footing about their dens,
that they may not be found out, and so should a wise man in the case of
his retirement. When the door is open, the thief passes it by as not
worth his while; but when it is bolted and sealed, it is a temptation
for people to be prying. To have it said “that such a one is never
out of his study, and sees nobody,” etc.; this furnishes matter for
discourse. He that makes his retirement too strict and severe, does as
good as call company to take notice of it.
neutral_text:
on the same topic:

If you want to protect your personal time and space, don't make a big show of it. When you're too obvious about guarding your privacy or isolating yourself, you actually draw more attention and curiosity. Think about it - if someone's always posting about needing "me time" or constantly talking about how they never go out anymore, people get intrigued and start asking questions. The best way to maintain your boundaries is to be subtle about it. Don't announce your need for solitude or make it seem like some dramatic lifestyle choice. Just quietly carve out the space you need without broadcasting it to everyone. When you're too rigid or vocal about protecting your alone time, you end up creating gossip and making people want to know what you're hiding. Keep it low-key, and most people won't even notice or care about your need for privacy.

stoic_text:
Now, to show you (says our author) how much earnester my entrance upon
philosophy was than my progress, my tutor Sotion gave me a wonderful
kindness for Pythagoras, and after him for Sextius: the former forbore
shedding of blood upon his _metempsychosis:_ and put men in fear of it,
lest they should offer violence to the souls of some of their departed
friends or relations. “Whether,” says he, “there be a transmigration or
not; if it be true, there is no hurt; if false, there is frugality: and
nothing is gotten by cruelty neither, but the cozening a wolf, perhaps,
or a vulture, of a supper.”
neutral_text:
Now, let me tell you how passionate I was about philosophy when I first started (way more than I am now, honestly). My teacher Sotion got me really into Pythagoras and later Sextius, who were both vegetarians. Pythagoras believed in reincarnation - that animal souls might actually be our dead relatives coming back in new bodies - so he thought eating meat was basically potential cannibalism.

Here's how he put it: "Look, maybe reincarnation is real, maybe it isn't. If it's true, then you're avoiding something terrible. If it's false, you're still saving money on groceries and being more environmentally conscious. Plus, the only thing you're really missing out on is competing with scavengers like wolves and vultures for dead flesh - not exactly a loss."

The logic was pretty compelling: it's a win-win situation where you either avoid spiritual disaster or just live more ethically and economically, with zero real downside.

stoic_text:
To descend to the particular branches and varieties would be
unnecessary and endless. There is a stubborn, a vindictive, a
quarrelsome, a violent, a froward, a sullen, a morose kind of anger;
and then we have this variety in complication too. One goes no
further than words; another proceeds immediately to blows, without a
word speaking; a third sort breaks out into cursing and reproachful
language; and there are that content themselves with chiding and
complaining. There is a conciliable anger and there is an implacable;
but in what form or degree soever it appears, all anger, without
exception, is vicious.
neutral_text:
Looking at all the different ways people get angry would take forever and honestly, it's pretty depressing. Some people are just naturally stubborn and hold grudges, others are always looking for a fight or ready to blow up at the smallest thing. Some folks get moody and sulky, while others turn into total jerks when they're mad.

And here's the thing - everyone expresses their anger differently too. Some people are all talk and will just yell at you, others skip the talking and go straight to getting physical. Then you've got the ones who turn into complete savages with their words, cursing you out and tearing you down. Others just whine and complain endlessly about everything that's bothering them.

Sure, some people can let it go and move on pretty quickly, but others? They'll hold onto that anger forever and never forgive you. But here's what I've learned: no matter how someone shows their anger or what triggers it, letting anger control you is always going to mess up your life and relationships. It just never leads anywhere good.

stoic_text:
Now, to sum up what is already delivered, we have showed what happiness
is, and wherein it consists: that it is founded upon wisdom and virtue;
for we must first know what we ought to do, and then live according to
that knowledge. We have also discoursed the helps of philosophy and
precept toward a _happy life_; the blessing of a good conscience; that
a good man can never be miserable, nor a wicked man happy; nor any
man unfortunate that cheerfully submits to Providence. We shall now
examine, how it comes to pass that, when the certain way to happiness
lies so fair before us, men will yet steer their course on the other
side, which as manifestly leads to ruin.
neutral_text: Now, to sum up what we've covered, we've shown what happiness is and how to get it: it's built on self-awareness and making good choices. You need to figure out what actually matters to you, then align your actions with those values. We've also talked about how philosophy and good advice can guide you toward a fulfilling life, the peace that comes from living with integrity, and how people who treat others well tend to be happier while those who don't often end up miserable. Plus, accepting life's ups and downs with a positive attitude makes you more resilient than constantly fighting what you can't change. Now let's dig into why, even when the path to happiness seems pretty straightforward, so many people still make choices that obviously lead them toward stress, conflict, and dissatisfaction instead.

stoic_text:
The particularity does much, but yet the same thing may receive
a different estimate from several persons; for there are ways of
marking and recommending it in such a manner, that if the same _good
office_ be done to twenty people, every one of them shall reckon
himself peculiarly obliged as a cunning whore, if she has a thousand
sweethearts, will persuade every one of them she loves him best. But
this is rather the artifice of conversation than the virtue of it.
neutral_text:
with mainstream reasoning:

Personalization matters a lot, but the same gesture can feel completely different to different people depending on how you present it. There are ways to frame and deliver your help so that even if you do the exact same favor for twenty different people, each one will feel like they got special treatment - kind of like how a manipulative person might make all their romantic interests feel like "the one." But honestly, this crosses the line from being socially skilled into being fake and calculating. 

The key is finding the sweet spot where you're genuinely connecting with people individually without being dishonest about it. Real relationship-building means actually caring about what makes each person unique, not just knowing which buttons to push to make them feel special.

stoic_text:
But “may not an honest man then be allowed to be angry at the murder
of his father, or the ravishing of his sister or daughter before his
face?” No, not at all. I will defend my parents, and I will repay the
injuries that are done them; but it is my piety and not my anger, that
moves me to it. I will do my duty without fear or confusion, I will not
rage, I will not weep; but discharge the office of a good man without
forfeiting the dignity of a man. If my father be assaulted, I will
endeavor to rescue him; if he be killed, I will do right to his memory;
find all this, not in any transport of passion, but in honor and
conscience. Neither is there any need of anger where reason does the
same thing.
neutral_text:
Look, I get it — when someone hurts your family, your first instinct is to see red and lose your mind. But here's the thing: getting furious actually makes you less effective at handling the situation.

When someone attacks your loved ones, absolutely step up and defend them. Get justice for them. But do it with a clear head, not while you're blinded by rage. Think about it — when you're angry, you make mistakes. You say things you regret, you might escalate the situation unnecessarily, or you could even put yourself in legal jeopardy.

Instead, channel that protective energy into smart action. If someone's threatening your family, call the police, document everything, get witnesses. If something's already happened, work with lawyers, push for proper prosecution, make sure the system does its job. You can be absolutely determined and relentless without being consumed by anger.

The goal is results, right? You want to protect your family and make sure justice happens. Well, you're way more likely to achieve that when you're thinking strategically instead of just reacting emotionally. Plus, your family needs you to be their rock during crisis — they need someone who's strong and reliable, not someone who's falling apart with rage.

stoic_text:
What can be happier than the condition both of mind and of fortune from
which we cannot fall—what can be a greater felicity than in a covetous,
designing age, for a roan to live safe among informers and thieves? It
puts a poor man into the very condition of Providence, that gives all,
without reserving anything to itself. How happy is he that owes nothing
but to himself, and only that which he can easily refuse or easily
pay! I do not reckon him poor that has but a little, but he is so that
covets more—it is a fair degree of plenty to have what is necessary.
Whether had a man better find satiety in want, or hunger in plenty? It
is not the augmenting of our fortunes, but the abating of our appetites
that makes us rich.
neutral_text:
on the same topic:

Want to know what real happiness looks like? It's having enough that you're not constantly worried about losing it all. In our culture of endless hustle and comparison, there's something incredibly freeing about living within your means and not getting caught up in everyone else's schemes to get ahead. Think about it—when you're content with less, you've basically got superpowers. You're not stressed about keeping up appearances or making payments on stuff you can't afford.

The sweet spot is when you owe money only to people who actually matter to you, and only amounts you can realistically handle. Here's the thing: being "poor" isn't about your bank account—it's about that nagging feeling that you need more, more, more. Having "enough" is actually pretty awesome when you think about it. 

Would you rather feel satisfied with what you've got, or constantly hungry for the next thing even when you're supposedly "successful"? Real wealth isn't about making more money—it's about wanting less stuff. When you dial down your expectations, everything else feels like a bonus.

stoic_text:
There is an end of the contest when one side deserts it; so that
the paying of anger with benefits puts a period to the controversy.
But, however, if it be our fortune to transgress, let not our anger
descend to the children, friends or relations, even of our bitterest
enemies. The very cruelty of Sylla was heightened by that instance of
incapacitating the issue of the proscribed. It is inhuman to entail the
hatred we have for the father upon his posterity.
neutral_text:
The best way to end a feud is when someone decides to stop fighting back. Responding to anger with kindness actually shuts down the whole conflict cycle. But if you do mess up and hurt someone, don't take it out on their kids, friends, or family members. Even dictators like Sulla made themselves look worse by punishing the children of their enemies. It's just wrong to hate someone's family because of issues you have with them.

Look, dragging innocent people into your beef makes you the bad guy, no matter how justified your original anger was. Keep your conflicts between the actual people involved. Their loved ones didn't do anything to you, and going after them will only make you look petty and cruel. Plus, it usually just creates more enemies and extends the drama way longer than it needs to go on.

stoic_text:
He that robs upon the highway had rather find his booty than force
it; ask any of them that live upon rapine, fraud, oppression, if they
had not rather enjoy a fortune honestly gotten, and their consciences
will not suffer them to deny it. Men are vicious only for the proof of
villainy; for at the same time that they commit it they condemn it;
nay, so powerful is virtue, and so gracious is Providence, that every
man has a light set up within him for a guide, which we do, all of
us, both see and acknowledge, though we do not pursue it. This it is
that makes the prisoner upon the torture happier than the executioner,
and sickness better than health, if we bear it without yielding or
repining—this it is that overcomes ill-fortune and moderates good—for
it marches betwixt the one and the other, with an equal contempt for
both. It turns (like fire) all things into itself, our actions and our
friendships are tinctured with it, and whatever it touches becomes
amiable.
neutral_text:
Even criminals know deep down they're doing wrong. Ask any thief or scammer if they wouldn't rather have earned their money legitimately, and they'll admit they would. People only act badly to prove something to themselves or others, but even while they're doing it, they feel guilty about it.

We all have an inner moral compass—call it conscience, intuition, or gut feeling—that tells us right from wrong. We can all sense it, even if we don't always follow it. That's why someone suffering through a difficult situation with grace and dignity often feels better about themselves than someone causing that suffering. It's why handling setbacks well can actually make you stronger, and why staying grounded during good times keeps you balanced.

Your character and values shape everything you do. They influence how you treat people, what kind of friendships you build, and how others see you. When you live according to your principles, it creates a positive ripple effect that touches every part of your life. People are drawn to authenticity and integrity—it makes you more likeable, trustworthy, and respected. Living with strong moral character isn't just about doing the right thing; it's about creating a life that feels genuinely good from the inside out.

stoic_text:
We are here to encounter the most outrageous, brutal, dangerous, and
intractable of all passions; the most loathsome and unmannerly; nay,
the most ridiculous too; and the subduing of this monster will do a
great deal toward the establishment of human peace. It is the method of
_physicians_ to begin with a description of the disease, before they
meddle with the cure: and I know not why this may not do as well in the
distempers of the mind as in those of the body.
neutral_text:
We're about to tackle one of the most destructive, ugly, and frankly embarrassing emotions out there. Getting control of this beast will seriously improve your relationships and overall quality of life. Just like doctors start by understanding what they're dealing with before jumping into treatment, it makes sense to really examine this mental pattern before we try to fix it.

Look, this emotion doesn't just hurt others - it makes you look bad too. It's one of those things that can completely sabotage your personal and professional relationships if you don't get a handle on it. The good news is that once you understand how it works and why it's so toxic, you can start building healthier ways to respond. Think of it like going to therapy or working with a coach - you've got to diagnose the problem before you can solve it. And trust me, putting in the work to overcome this will pay dividends in every area of your life.

stoic_text:
Alexander was possessed with the madness of laying kingdoms waste.
He began with Greece, where he was brought up; and there he quarried
himself upon that in it which was the best; he enslaved Lacedemon, and
silenced Athens: nor was he content with the destruction of those towns
which his father Philip had either conquered or bought; but he made
himself the enemy of human nature; and, like the worst of beasts, he
worried what he could not eat.
neutral_text: Alexander was driven by toxic ambition and unchecked ego. He started by turning on his own people in Greece, where he'd grown up - talk about biting the hand that feeds you. He crushed Sparta and silenced Athens, not satisfied with just building on what his father Philip had already accomplished. Instead of being grateful for his advantages, he developed a destructive need to dominate everyone around him. Like someone with serious anger issues, he lashed out and destroyed things even when there was nothing to gain from it - pure waste and cruelty for its own sake.

stoic_text:
There is a great difference betwixt a common obligation and a
particular; he that lends my country money, obliges me only as a part
of the whole. Plato crossed the river, and the ferry-man would take no
money of him: he reflected upon it as honor done to himself; and told
him, “That Plato was in debt.” But Plato, when he found it to be no
more than he did for others, recalled his words, “For,” says he, “Plato
will owe nothing in particular for a benefit in common; what I owe with
others, I will pay with others.”
neutral_text:
using mainstream reasoning:

There's a huge difference between getting special treatment and just being part of a group deal. When someone does something nice for your whole community - like a local business owner sponsoring the town festival - you shouldn't feel personally indebted to them. 

Take this example: A famous philosopher got a free ferry ride and initially thought the ferryman was showing him personal respect, so he felt he owed the guy a favor. But then he realized the ferryman gave free rides to everyone that day as a community gesture. So he changed his tune and said, "Actually, I don't owe you anything special - this was just a general nice thing you did for everyone."

The takeaway? Don't let people make you feel guilty or obligated when they're just being generally nice to a whole group. Save your sense of personal debt for times when someone goes out of their way specifically for you. Otherwise, you'll exhaust yourself trying to pay back every random act of kindness that wasn't even meant to single you out.

stoic_text:
Provided that we look to our consciences, no matter for opinion: let
me deserve well, though I hear ill. The common people take stomach and
audacity for the marks of magnanimity and honor; and if a man be soft
and modest, they look upon him as an easy fop; but when they come once
to observe the dignity of his mind in the equality and firmness of his
actions; and that his external quiet is founded upon an internal peace,
the very same people who have him in esteem and admiration; for there
is no man but approves of virtue, though but few pursue it; we see
where it is, but we dare not venture to come at it: and the reason is,
we overvalue that which we must quit to obtain it.
neutral_text: As long as you know you're doing the right thing, don't worry too much about what people initially think. Focus on being genuinely good, even if some people misunderstand you at first. Most people mistake loud, aggressive behavior for real confidence and success. If you're quiet and humble, they might write you off as weak or unimpressive. But here's the thing - when people actually see you in action over time, when they notice how consistent and solid you are, how your calm exterior comes from real inner confidence, those same people will end up respecting and admiring you. Everyone recognizes and appreciates authentic goodness when they see it, even if most people don't have the courage to live that way themselves. We can spot genuine character from a mile away, but we're often too scared to commit to it because we think we'd have to give up too much of what we currently have or want.

stoic_text:
Lucilius tells me, that he was written to by a friend, but cautions
me withal not to say anything to him of the affair in question; for
he himself stands upon the same guard. What is this but to affirm and
to deny the same thing in the same breath, in calling a man a friend,
whom we dare not trust as our own soul? For there must be no reserves
in friendship: as much deliberation as you please before the league
is struck, but no doubtings or jealousies after. It is a preposterous
weakness to love a man before we know him, and not to care for him
after. It requires time to consider of a friendship, but the resolution
once taken, entitles him to my very heart. I look upon my thoughts to
be as safe in his breast as in my own: I shall, without any scruple,
make him the confidant of my most secret cares and counsels.
neutral_text:
Look, here's the thing about friendship that I learned the hard way: you can't have it both ways. Either someone is truly your friend, or they're just an acquaintance you're being polite to.

I had a buddy tell me about this situation where someone reached out to him but then immediately said "don't tell anyone about this." Red flag right there. If you're calling someone your friend but you can't actually trust them with real information, what kind of friendship is that really?

Here's my take: real friendship means you can be vulnerable with each other. Yes, you should definitely take your time getting to know someone before you open up completely - don't overshare with people you barely know. But once you've decided someone has earned your trust and proven themselves reliable, go all in. Share your real thoughts, your actual problems, the stuff that matters.

The worst thing you can do is get stuck in that weird middle ground where you call someone a friend but still keep them at arm's length. That's not protecting yourself - that's just wasting everyone's time. Either invest in the relationship properly, or acknowledge it's more casual than you're pretending it is.

stoic_text:
How vain a thing is it for us to enter upon projects, and to say to
ourselves, “Well, I will go build, purchase, discharge such offices,
settle my affairs, and then retire!” We are all of us born to the same
casualties—all equally frail and uncertain of to-morrow. At the very
altar where we pray for life, we learn to die, by seeing the sacrifices
killed before us. But there is no need of a wound, or searching the
heart for it, when the noose of a cord, or the smothering of a pillow
will do the work. All things have their seasons—they begin, they
increase, and they die. The heavens and the earth grow old, and are
appointed their periods.
neutral_text: How pointless it is to keep putting off the things that really matter, telling ourselves, "Once I get that promotion, buy the house, pay off my debts, get the kids through college, THEN I'll start living the life I want!" We're all vulnerable to the same risks—heart attacks, accidents, cancer can strike anyone at any time. Every news story about someone dying unexpectedly should remind us how fragile we really are. And honestly, you don't need some dramatic tragedy—stress, burnout, or just the slow grind of unfulfilling years can kill your spirit just as effectively. Everything in life has an expiration date—relationships end, careers plateau, our bodies break down, even civilizations rise and fall. The clock is always ticking.

stoic_text:
To conclude, where my proper virtue fails me, I will have recourse to
examples, and say to myself, Am I greater than Philip or Augustus, who
both of them put up with greater reproaches? Many have pardoned their
enemies, and shall not I forgive a neglect, a little freedom of the
tongue? Nay, the patience but of a second thought does the business:
for though the first shock be violent; take it in parts, and it is
subdued. And, to wind up all in one word, the great lesson of mankind,
as well in this as in all other cases, is, “to do as we would be done
by.”
neutral_text:
To wrap this up, when I'm struggling to let things go, I'll remind myself of successful people who've dealt with way worse criticism and came out on top. Look at any CEO or public figure - they face brutal attacks daily but stay focused on their goals. If they can handle that level of heat, surely I can deal with someone being rude or dismissive to me.

Here's the thing: that initial sting when someone disrespects you? It's totally normal to feel that. But if you just give yourself a moment to cool down instead of reacting immediately, you'll realize it's usually not worth the drama. Break it down - what actually happened versus what your emotions are telling you happened.

And honestly, the golden rule still works: treat people how you want to be treated. When you mess up, you want forgiveness and understanding, right? Same goes for everyone else. Most people aren't trying to hurt you personally - they're just dealing with their own stuff.

stoic_text:
We may enlarge, indeed, or contract, according to the circumstances
of time, place, or abilities; but above all things we must be sure to
keep ourselves in action, for he that is slothful is dead even while
he lives. Was there ever any state so desperate as that of Athens
under the thirty tyrants—where it was capital to be honest, and the
senate-house was turned into a college of hangmen? Never was any
government so wretched and so hopeless; and yet Socrates at the same
time preached _temperance_ to the _tyrants_, and courage to the rest,
and afterwards died an eminent example of faith and resolution, and a
sacrifice for the common good.
neutral_text:
You've got to stay flexible with your goals—scale them up or down based on what's realistic given your situation, resources, and abilities. But the most important thing? Keep moving forward. Don't let yourself fall into that trap of endless planning without doing anything. People who stay on the sidelines watching life pass by might as well be dead.

Look at extreme situations throughout history. Take Athens under those brutal tyrants—it was literally dangerous to be a decent person, and the government was basically a death squad. Things couldn't have looked more hopeless. But even then, people like Socrates didn't give up. He kept speaking truth to power, encouraging others to stay brave, and ultimately put his life on the line for what mattered. 

The point is, if someone could make a difference in those impossible circumstances, you can definitely make progress in yours. No matter how bad things seem, there's always something you can do to move the needle forward.

stoic_text:
The humanity and excellence of this virtue is confessed at all hands,
as well by the men of _pleasure_, and those that think every man was
made for himself, as by the Stoics, that make “man a sociable creature,
and born for the common good of mankind:” for it is of all dispositions
the most _peaceable_ and _quiet_. But before we enter any farther upon
the discourse, it should be first known what _clemency_ is, that we may
distinguish it from _pity_; which is a _weakness_, though many times
mistaken for a _virtue_: and the next thing will be, to bring the mind
to the _habit_ and _exercise_ of it.
neutral_text: Everyone agrees that being merciful and forgiving is a great quality - whether you're someone who prioritizes your own happiness or someone who believes we should all work together for society's benefit. It's one of the most calming and peaceful traits you can develop. But before we dive deeper into this topic, we need to get clear on what real mercy actually looks like, so we don't confuse it with pity - which might feel virtuous but is actually just emotional weakness that doesn't help anyone. Once we understand the difference, we can focus on how to actually practice healthy compassion in our daily lives.

stoic_text:
The injuries that press hardest upon us are those which either we
have not deserved, or not expected, or, at least, not in so high a
degree. This arises from the love of ourselves: for every man takes
upon him, like a prince, in this case, to practice all liberties, and
to allow none, which proceeds either from ignorance or insolence. What
news is it for people to do ill things? for an enemy to hurt us; nay,
for a friend or a servant to transgress, and to prove treacherous,
ungrateful, covetous, impious? What we find in one man we may in
another, and there is more security in fortune than in men. Our joys
are mingled with fear, and a tempest may arise out of a calm; but a
skilful pilot is always provided for it.
neutral_text:
The betrayals that hurt us most are the ones we didn't see coming, didn't think we deserved, or that felt way more intense than we expected. This happens because we're naturally self-protective - we all tend to cut ourselves slack while holding others to impossibly high standards, which sets us up for disappointment every time.

But really, why are we so shocked when people act badly? Your enemy is going to try to hurt you - that's what enemies do. Even friends, family, and coworkers will sometimes lie, cheat, steal, or throw you under the bus. If you've seen it happen once, you know it can happen again. People are unpredictable and messy, while at least with random bad luck you know it's nothing personal.

The good times always come with some anxiety mixed in, and even when everything seems perfect, drama can explode out of nowhere. The key is to stay prepared for whatever comes your way - kind of like a good captain who always keeps an eye on the weather, even on sunny days.

stoic_text:
There are seasons wherein a benefit is neither to be received nor
requited. To press a return upon me when I do not desire it, is
unmannerly; but it is worse to force me to desire it. How rigorous
would he be to exact a requital; who is thus eager to return it! To
wish a man in distress that I may relieve him, is first to wish him
miserable: to wish that he may stand in need of anybody, is _against
him_; and to wish that he may stand in need of me, is _for myself_:
so that my business is not so much a charity to my friend as the
cancelling of a bond; nay, it is half-way the wish of an enemy. It is
barbarous to wish a man in chains, slavery, or want, only to bring
him out again: let me rather wish him powerful and happy, and myself
indebted to him! By nature we are prone to mercy, humanity compassion;
may we be excited to be more so by the number of the grateful! may
their number increase, and may we have no need of trying them!
neutral_text:
There's a right time and place for giving and receiving help. When someone insists on paying you back when you don't want them to, it's pushy and awkward - but it's even worse to pressure someone into feeling like they owe you something. Anyone who's that desperate to return a favor would probably be a nightmare to deal with if they expected payback from you!

Here's the thing: secretly hoping your friends run into problems so you can swoop in and help them isn't actually being a good friend - it's pretty messed up when you think about it. Wishing someone would need anybody's help is hoping for their suffering. And if you're specifically hoping they'll need YOUR help? That's just selfish - you're not really trying to be charitable, you're trying to put them in your debt or make yourself feel important. That's honestly kind of toxic behavior.

It's cruel to want people to struggle with money problems, relationship drama, or other hardships just so you can be the hero who saves the day. Instead, hope your friends are thriving and successful - even if that means you end up being the one who occasionally needs their support! We're naturally wired to want to help each other out, so let's focus on building relationships with genuinely grateful people who appreciate kindness. The goal should be creating a world where fewer people need rescuing in the first place.

stoic_text:
Nay, Providence itself is gracious to the wicked posterity of an
honorable race. The counsels of heaven are guided by wisdom, mercy, and
justice. Some men are made kings of their proper virtues, without any
respect to their predecessors: others for their ancestors’ sakes, whose
virtues, though neglected in their lives, come to be afterward rewarded
in their issues. And it is but equity, that our gratitude should extend
as far as the influence of their heroical actions and examples.
neutral_text:
Look, sometimes life seems unfair when you see bad people getting ahead, but there's usually more to the story. Even when someone doesn't deserve their success, there are often good reasons behind it that we can't see.

Sometimes people earn their position through genuine merit and hard work - they've actually got the skills and character to back it up. Other times, they benefit from their family's legacy and connections. And honestly? That's not always wrong. When previous generations built something meaningful, contributed to society, or set a positive example, it makes sense that their descendants might get some advantages from that.

Think of it this way - we all benefit from the good things our parents and grandparents did, whether that's the values they taught us, the sacrifices they made, or the reputation they built. It's actually pretty reasonable that society continues to honor and reward families who've made lasting positive contributions. It encourages people to think beyond just themselves and consider the legacy they're leaving behind.

stoic_text:
The burning of Lyons may serve to show us that we are never safe, and
to arm us against all surprises. The terror of it must needs be great,
for the calamity is almost without example. If it had been fired by an
enemy, the flame would have left some further mischief to have been
done by the soldiers; but to be wholly consumed, we have not heard of
many earthquakes so pernicious: so many rarities to be destroyed in
one night; and in the depth of peace to suffer an outrage beyond the
extremity of war; who would believe it? but twelve hours betwixt so
fair a city and none at all! It was laid in ashes in less time than it
would require to tell the story.
neutral_text:
The fire that destroyed Lyons should be a wake-up call that disasters can strike anywhere, anytime. We need to prepare for the unexpected and build resilience into our lives. The scale of devastation was unimaginable - even a military attack would have left something standing, but this total destruction was worse than most natural disasters. To see so much history and culture wiped out overnight, and during peacetime no less - it's the kind of thing that seems impossible until it happens. Just twelve hours between having a thriving city and having nothing at all! The whole place was reduced to ash faster than you could even process what was happening.

This is why emergency preparedness matters. Keep important documents backed up, maintain insurance coverage, and have an emergency fund. Know your evacuation routes and have a family disaster plan. The unthinkable can and does happen - floods, fires, economic crashes, health crises. Don't live in fear, but do live prepared. Build diverse income streams, maintain strong relationships, and develop adaptable skills. When catastrophe strikes, it's too late to start planning.

stoic_text:
That which we call _death_ is but a pause or suspension; and, in
truth, a progress to life, only our thoughts look downward upon the
body, and not forward upon things to come. All things under the sun
are mortal—cities—empires—and the time will come when it shall be a
question where they were, and, perchance, whether ever they had a
being or not. Some will be destroyed by war, others by luxury, fire,
inundations, earthquakes—why should it trouble me then to die, as a
forerunner of an universal dissolution? A great mind submits itself to
God, and suffers willingly what the law of the universe will otherwise
bring to pass upon necessity.
neutral_text:
Look, death is really just the next chapter in your journey—think of it as a transition rather than an ending. We get so caught up focusing on what's happening to our physical bodies that we forget to consider the bigger picture and what might come next.

Everything around us has an expiration date—entire civilizations, governments, even the landmarks we think will last forever. Historians are still debating whether some ancient societies even existed at all. Some get wiped out by wars, others collapse from corruption, natural disasters, economic crashes—it's all part of the cycle.

So why stress about your own mortality when it's just part of this massive, universal process that everything goes through? The key is developing a mature perspective that accepts life's natural rhythms instead of fighting against them. When you embrace the flow of life rather than resisting it, you'll find peace with the inevitable changes that are coming whether you like it or not.

stoic_text:
Asiaticus Valerius, (one of Caligula’s particular friends, and a man of
stomach, that would not easily digest an affront) Caligula told him in
public what kind of bedfellow his wife was. Good God! that ever any man
should hear this, or a prince speak it, especially to a man of consular
authority, a friend, and a husband: and in such a manner too as at once
to own his disgust and his adultery. The tribune Chæreas had a weak
broken voice, like an hermaphrodite; when he came to Caligula for the
_word_, he would give him sometimes _Venus_, otherwhiles _Priapus_,
as a slur upon him both ways. Valerius was afterwards the principal
instrument in the conspiracy against him; and Chæreas, to convince him
of his manhood, at one blow cleft him down the chin with his sword.
No man was so forward as Caligula to _break_ a jest, and no man so
unwilling to _bear_ it.
neutral_text:
Look at this guy Valerius, one of Caligula's close friends who normally had a backbone. Caligula publicly humiliated him by bragging about sleeping with his wife - can you imagine? No decent person should have to put up with that kind of disrespect, especially from someone who's supposed to be your friend. And there was this tribune Chæreas who had a high voice, so Caligula would mock him with sexual nicknames every chance he got. 

Here's the thing - when people repeatedly cross your boundaries and disrespect you like this, you can't just take it forever. Your mental health and self-respect are too important. Both Valerius and Chæreas eventually had enough and took revenge by assassinating Caligula. Now I'm not saying violence is the answer, but the lesson here is clear: bullies who dish it out but can't take it themselves usually end up facing consequences. Don't let toxic people walk all over you - stand up for yourself, set firm boundaries, and remove destructive people from your life before the situation explodes.

stoic_text:
“Here follows now,” says Tacitus, “the death of Seneca, to Nero’s great
satisfaction; not so much for any pregnant proof against him that he
was of Piso’s conspiracy; but Nero was resolved to do that by the sword
which he could not effect by poison. For it is reported, that Nero
had corrupted Cleonicus (a freeman of Seneca’s) to give his master
poison, which did not succeed. Whether that the servant had discovered
it to his master, or that Seneca, by his own caution and jealousy, had
avoided it; for he lived only upon a simple diet, as the fruits of the
earth, and his drink was most commonly river water.
neutral_text:
of the same topic (surviving threats from those in power):

"When you're dealing with someone powerful who wants to harm you," says the expert, "you need to be smart about protecting yourself. Nero clearly had it out for Seneca - not because he had real evidence of wrongdoing, but because he felt threatened by him. The emperor had already tried having Seneca poisoned through a bribed servant, but that failed. Maybe the servant got cold feet and warned Seneca, or maybe Seneca was just naturally suspicious and careful about what he consumed. Either way, Seneca had developed good safety habits - he kept his diet simple, mostly eating basic foods and drinking plain water.

The lesson here? When someone with power has targeted you, don't ignore the warning signs. Build protective routines into your life, stay alert to potential threats, and don't make yourself an easy target. Sometimes being paranoid isn't paranoia - it's survival. Trust your instincts, keep your habits unpredictable, and remember that powerful people who feel threatened can be incredibly dangerous."

stoic_text:
It is a wicked world, and we make part of it; and the way to be quiet
is to bear one with another. “Such a man,” we cry, “has done me a
shrewd turn, and I never did him any hurt.” Well, but it may be I have
mischieved other people, or at least, I may live to do as much to him
as that comes to. “Such a one has spoken ill things of me;” but if I
first speak ill of him, as I do of many others, this is not an injury,
but a repayment. What if he did overshoot himself? He was loth to lose
his conceit perhaps, but there was no malice in it; and if he had
not done me a mischief, he must have done himself one. How many good
offices are there that look like injuries! Nay, how many have been
reconciled and good friends after a professed hatred!
neutral_text:
Look, people are messy and imperfect, and we're all part of the same flawed human experience. The key to staying sane is cutting everyone some slack. 

When someone screws you over and you're thinking "I never did anything to them!" - hold up. You've probably hurt other people without realizing it, or you might end up doing something equally crappy to them down the road. And when someone talks trash about you, remember you've probably gossiped about plenty of others. They're just giving you a taste of your own medicine.

Maybe they didn't even mean to hurt you - they were just being careless or trying to save face. If they hadn't messed with you, they probably would've found another way to shoot themselves in the foot anyway. Plus, sometimes what feels like a betrayal actually turns out to be helpful in disguise.
