Here's the thing: tons of people who used to hate each other's guts end up becoming close friends. Conflict doesn't have to be permanent, and holding grudges just keeps you stuck in negativity when you could be moving forward.

stoic_text:
If the professors of virtue be as the world calls them, avaricious,
libidinous, ambitious—what are they then that have a detestation for
the very name of it: but malicious natures do not want wit to abuse
honester men than themselves. It is the practice of the multitude to
bark at eminent men as little dogs do at strangers; for they look upon
other men’s virtues as the upbraiding of their own wickedness. We
should do well to commend those that are good, if not, let us pass them
over; but, however, let us spare ourselves: for beside the blaspheming
of virtue, our rage is to no purpose. But to return now to my text.
neutral_text:
Look, if even the people who teach about doing good are greedy, sleazy, and power-hungry—imagine how bitter the people who openly hate goodness must be. Mean-spirited people are actually pretty clever at tearing down anyone better than them. It's like how small dogs yap at every stranger—most people attack successful, moral people because it makes them feel less guilty about their own poor choices. 

Here's what I think we should do: celebrate good people when we can, and if we can't bring ourselves to praise them, at least just ignore them. Either way, we need to protect our own peace of mind. Getting worked up and trash-talking decent people doesn't accomplish anything except making us look petty and bitter. It's honestly just a waste of our energy and mental space. Anyway, let me get back to my main point.

stoic_text:
We are ready enough to limit others but loth to put bonds and
restraints upon ourselves, though we know that many times a greater
evil is cured by a less; and the mind that will not be brought to
virtue by precepts, comes to it frequently by necessity. Let us try a
little to eat upon a joint stool, to serve ourselves, to live within
compass, and accommodate our clothes to the end they were made for.
Occasional experiments of our moderation give us the best proof of
our firmness and virtue. A well-governed appetite is a great part of
liberty, and it is a blessed lot, that since no man can have all things
that he would have, we may all of us forbear desiring what we have not.
It is the office of temperance to overrule us in our pleasures; some
she rejects, others she qualifies and keeps within bounds. Oh! the
delights of rest when a man comes to be weary, and of meat when he is
heartily hungry.
neutral_text:
We're quick to tell others what they should cut back on, but hate putting limits on ourselves—even though we know that sometimes short-term discomfort leads to long-term benefits. If you can't motivate yourself to change through willpower alone, try creating situations where you don't have a choice. 

Challenge yourself to live simply for a while: eat basic meals, do your own chores, stick to a tight budget, wear practical clothes instead of always buying new ones. These little experiments show you what you're actually capable of and build real confidence.

Learning to control your impulses is incredibly freeing—it means you're not constantly at the mercy of every craving or want. And here's the thing: since you can't have everything anyway, you might as well get good at being okay without it. The key is finding balance with your pleasures—cut out some entirely, and enjoy others in moderation.

Trust me, nothing beats the satisfaction of rest when you're truly tired, or a good meal when you're genuinely hungry. When you stop constantly indulging, the simple pleasures hit so much harder.

stoic_text:
But without any further alteration of dialogue, the conclusion is this;
if you meant him the kindness, he is answerable for it, and I may enjoy
the fruit of it without being obliged by it: but if it was done for
my sake, then I am accountable; or howsoever, upon any occasion, I am
ready to do you all the kind offices imaginable; not as the return of
a benefit, but as the earnest of a friendship; which you are not to
challenge neither, but to entertain as an act of honor and of justice,
rather than of gratitude. If a man find the body of my dead father in
a desert, and give it a burial; if he did it as to my father, I am
beholden to him: but if the body was unknown to him, and that he would
have done the same thing for any other body, I am no farther concerned
in it than as a piece of public humanity.
neutral_text:
Here's the modern advice version:

Look, here's the bottom line: you need to understand people's real motivations before you feel obligated to them. If someone did you a favor because they genuinely cared about helping you personally, then yeah, you should probably return the kindness when you can. But if they were just doing what they'd do for anyone, or if they had their own reasons that had nothing to do with you, don't stress about "owing" them.

Either way, I'm always down to help people out - not because I feel like I have to pay anyone back, but because that's how you build real friendships. Just don't expect me to feel guilty or keep a running tab of favors. Think of it as being a decent human being rather than some kind of debt repayment.

Like, if someone found my dad's body and buried it - if they knew it was my dad, I'd be grateful and want to help them out. But if it was just some stranger doing what any good person would do for any body they found? That's just basic human decency, and while I appreciate it, I'm not personally on the hook for it.

stoic_text:
Lysimachus, another of his friends, he exposed to a lion; and this
very Lysimachus, after he had escaped this danger, was never the more
merciful when he came to reign himself; for he cut off the ears and
nose of his friend Telesphorous; and when he had so disfigured him
that he had no longer the face of a man, he threw him into a dungeon,
and there kept him to be showed for a monster, as a strange sight.
The place was so low that he was fain to creep upon all fours, and
his sides were galled too with the straitness of it. In this misery
he lay half-famished in his own filth; so odious, so terrible, and
so loathsome a spectacle, that the horror of his condition had even
extinguished all pity for him. “Nothing was ever so unlike a mar as the
poor wretch that suffered this, saving the tyrant that acted it.”
neutral_text:
Look, here's the thing about bullies and abusers - they almost always end up becoming exactly what they once suffered under. Take this guy Lysimachus. He barely survived his boss throwing him to a literal lion, right? You'd think going through that nightmare would make him more compassionate, more understanding of what it's like to be powerless and terrorized.

But nope. The second he got power himself, he became just as cruel - maybe even worse. He tortured his own friend, mutilating him beyond recognition and keeping him in conditions so horrific that people lost all sympathy for the victim. The irony is devastating: the guy who survived being treated like an animal turned around and made someone else into something "so unlike a man."

This is why breaking cycles of abuse is so crucial. Trauma doesn't automatically make you a better person - sometimes it just teaches you new ways to hurt others. If you've been through hell, you have a choice: use that experience to develop empathy and protect others, or use it as a playbook for your own cruelty. The victim becoming the perpetrator is one of the most tragic patterns in human behavior, and it shows how power can corrupt even those who should know better.

stoic_text:
In civil and domestic affairs, a wise man may stand in need of
counsel, as of a physician, an advocate, a solicitor; but in greater
matters, the blessing of wise men rests in the joy they take in the
communication of their virtues. If there were nothing else in it, a
man would apply himself to wisdom, because it settles him in a perfect
tranquillity of mind.
neutral_text: In work and family situations, smart people know when to ask for help - whether that's getting medical advice, legal counsel, or professional services. But when it comes to the bigger picture of life, successful people find their greatest satisfaction in sharing their knowledge and skills with others. Even if mentoring and teaching didn't have any other benefits, people would still pursue expertise and personal growth simply because it gives them such incredible peace of mind and confidence in handling whatever life throws at them.

stoic_text:
This is undoubtedly the most detestable of vices, even compared with
the worst of them. Avarice scrapes and gathers together that which
somebody may be the better for: but anger lashes out, and no man comes
_off_ gratis. An angry master makes one servant run away, and another
hang himself; and his choler causes him a much greater loss than he
suffered in the occasion of it. It is the cause of mourning to the
father, and of divorce to the husband: it makes the magistrate odious,
and gives the candidate a repulse. And it is worse than luxury too,
which only aims at its proper pleasure; whereas the other is bent upon
another body’s pain.
neutral_text:
Anger is honestly one of the most destructive emotions you can have. Look, greed is bad - it's all about hoarding stuff for yourself - but at least someone might eventually benefit from what you've accumulated. Anger? It just destroys everything in its path, and everyone pays the price.

When you fly off the handle, you end up losing way more than whatever originally set you off. Think about toxic bosses - their anger drives away good employees and creates a workplace so miserable that people would rather quit than deal with it. Parents who can't control their temper damage their relationships with their kids forever. Couples break up because one person's anger makes living together unbearable. Even your career suffers - nobody wants to work with someone who has anger issues.

Here's the thing: anger hurts you more than whoever you're mad at. At least when someone's being selfish or indulgent, they're trying to make themselves feel good. But when you're angry, you're literally choosing to make yourself miserable while trying to make someone else miserable too. It's like drinking poison and hoping the other person gets sick.

stoic_text:
Of all felicities, the most charming is that of a _firm_ and _gentle
friendship_. It sweetens all our cares, dispels our sorrows, and
counsels us in all extremities. Nay, if there were no other comfort in
it than the bare exercise of so generous a virtue, even for that single
reason, a man would not be without it. Beside, that it is a sovereign
antidote against all calamities, even against the fear of death itself.
neutral_text:
Of all the good things in life, nothing beats having a **solid** and **caring friendship**. It makes all your problems feel lighter, cheers you up when you're down, and gives you someone to talk through tough decisions with. Honestly, even if friendship didn't help you practically, just being able to care deeply about someone else makes you feel good about yourself as a person.

Plus, having close friends is like the ultimate insurance policy against life's curveballs. When you know you've got people in your corner who really have your back, even scary stuff like aging or death doesn't feel as overwhelming. There's something about knowing you're not facing life alone that makes everything more manageable.

stoic_text:
But whatever we do, let us be sure always to keep a grateful mind.
It is not enough to say, what requital shall a poor man offer to a
prince; or a slave to his patron; when it is the glory of gratitude
that it depends only upon the good will? Suppose a man defends my
fame; delivers me from beggary; saves my life; or gives me liberty,
that is more than life; how shall I be grateful to that man? I will
receive, cherish, and rejoice in the benefit. Take it kindly, and
it is requited: not that the debt itself is discharged, but it is
nevertheless a discharge of the conscience. I will yet distinguish
betwixt the debtor that becomes insolvent by expenses upon whores and
dice, and another that is undone by fire or thieves; nor do I take this
gratitude for a payment, but there is no danger, I presume, of being
arrested for such a debt.
neutral_text:
focusing on the same topic of gratitude and reciprocity:

Look, gratitude isn't just about keeping score or paying people back dollar for dollar. Sometimes you can't match what someone did for you - like if your boss gives you a huge promotion or a friend helps you through a major crisis. The real power of gratitude is that it's about your attitude and appreciation, not your bank account.

When someone defends your reputation, helps you financially, saves your ass, or opens doors for you - how do you repay that? Simple: acknowledge it genuinely, let them know it mattered, and carry that positive energy forward. A heartfelt "thank you" and showing real appreciation often means more than trying to calculate some exact payback.

Now, there's a difference between someone who can't reciprocate because they blew their money on stupid stuff versus someone who's genuinely struggling through no fault of their own. But here's the thing - gratitude itself is valuable. It strengthens relationships, makes people want to help you again, and creates good karma. Nobody's going to sue you for an unpaid gratitude debt, but people remember who appreciates them and who doesn't. That emotional investment pays dividends in ways you can't always predict.

stoic_text:
A physician is not angry at the intemperance of a mad patient; nor does
he take it ill to be railed at by a man in a fever; just so should a
wise man treat all mankind as a physician does his patient; and looking
upon them only as sick and extravagant, let their words and actions,
whether good or bad, go equally for nothing, attending still his duty
even in the coarsest offices that may conduce to their recovery. Men
that are proud, froward, and powerful, he values their scorn as little
as their quality, and looks upon them no otherwise than as people in
the excess of a fever. If a beggar worships him, or if he takes no
notice of him, it is all one to him; and with a rich man he makes it
the same case. Their honors and their injuries he accounts much alike;
without rejoicing at the one, or grieving at the other.
neutral_text:
You know how doctors don't take it personally when sick patients lash out or act irrationally? They understand it's the illness talking, not the real person. We should adopt this same mindset with difficult people in our lives. When someone's being a jerk, try to see what's really going on underneath - maybe they're stressed, insecure, or dealing with their own problems. This perspective shift helps you stay calm and respond more effectively instead of getting triggered.

Don't let people's social status mess with your head either. That arrogant boss or rude wealthy person? Their behavior says more about their insecurities than your worth. And don't get too attached to praise from people you don't respect - if their criticism doesn't matter, neither should their compliments. Keep your emotional balance by remembering that most people's reactions to you are really about their own stuff. Focus on being helpful where you can, but don't tie your mood to how others treat you. This emotional detachment isn't about being cold - it's about staying centered so you can actually be more effective in your relationships and work.

stoic_text:
The common soldier where he cannot use his hands, fights with his
looks, his example, his encouragement, his voice, and stands his ground
even when he has lost his hands, and does service too with his very
clamor, so that in any condition whatsoever, he still discharges the
duty of a good patriot—nay, he that spends his time well even in a
retirement, gives a great example.
neutral_text:
Look, even when you can't contribute in your usual way, you can still make a difference. Maybe you're sidelined at work due to budget cuts, or dealing with health issues, or going through a tough personal situation. That doesn't mean you're useless.

You can still mentor younger colleagues, share your experience, offer emotional support to friends, or simply show others how to handle adversity with grace. Your attitude and resilience become your contribution. And honestly? Sometimes the person who steps back and focuses on their own growth and well-being sets just as powerful an example as the person charging ahead. People notice when someone handles setbacks well or uses downtime productively. Your comeback story could be exactly what someone else needs to see to believe in their own potential.

stoic_text:
It is a common thing to screw up justice to the pitch of an injury. A
man may be _over-righteous_; and why not _over-grateful_ too? There is
a mischievous excess, that borders so close upon ingratitude, that it
is no easy matter to distinguish the one from the other: but, in regard
that there is good-will in the bottom of it, (however distempered, for
it is effectually but kindness out of the wits,) we shall discourse it
under the title of _Gratitude mistaken_.
neutral_text:
on the same topic:

You know how some people take things way too far? Like, we all know someone who's so obsessed with "doing the right thing" that they become total jerks about it. Well, the same thing happens with gratitude - some folks get so intense about paying people back that it actually backfires.

Think about it: when someone does you a favor and you go completely overboard trying to repay them, it gets weird and uncomfortable for everyone. You might shower them with expensive gifts they don't want, or constantly bring up what they did for you, or insist on "evening the score" when they just wanted to be nice. At that point, your gratitude starts feeling more like a burden than appreciation.

The tricky part is that this kind of over-the-top behavior can look a lot like being ungrateful - both make the other person feel bad! But there's a key difference: the over-grateful person genuinely means well, they're just expressing it in a way that misses the mark. It's basically kindness gone haywire. So while it's annoying, we should probably cut these people some slack and recognize it as gratitude that's just... confused.

stoic_text:
I will never envy those that the people call great and happy. A sound
mind is not to be shaken with a popular and vain applause; nor is it
in the power of their pride to disturb the state of our happiness. An
honest man is known now-a-days by the dust he raises upon the way, and
it is become a point of honor to overrun people, and keep all at a
distance; though he that is put out of the way may perchance be happier
than he that takes it. He that would exercise a power profitable to
himself, and grievous to nobody else, let him practice it upon his
passion. They that have burnt cities, otherwise invincible, driven
armies before them, and bathed themselves in human blood, after they
have overcome all open enemies, they have been vanquished by their
lust, by their cruelty, and without any resistance.
neutral_text: I'll never be jealous of those celebrities and influencers everyone obsesses over. When you're confident in yourself, you don't need constant validation from likes and followers - their flashy lifestyle can't touch your inner peace. Success today seems to be all about stepping on others and creating drama for attention, even though the people getting pushed aside might actually be living better lives. If you want real power that actually improves your life without hurting anyone else, work on mastering your own bad habits and impulses. Look at all those powerful figures throughout history - they conquered nations and built empires, but in the end they were destroyed by their own addictions, toxic behavior, and lack of self-control. The enemies that really matter are the ones inside your own head.

stoic_text:
But as the faults of great men are not presently punished with thunder
from above, let them have a like regard to their inferiors here upon
earth. He that has revenge in his power, and does not use it, is the
great man. Which is the more beautiful and agreeable state, that of
a calm, a temperate, and a clear day; or that of lightning, thunder,
and tempests? and this is the very difference betwixt a moderate and
turbulent government. It is for low and vulgar spirits to brawl, storm,
and transport themselves: but it is not for the majesty of a prince to
lash out into intemperance of words. Some will think it rather slavery
than empire to be debarred liberty of speech: and what if it be, when
government itself is but a more illustrious servitude?
neutral_text:
Look, when you're in a position of power - whether you're a manager, parent, or just someone with influence - how you handle conflict says everything about your leadership style. Just because you *can* come down hard on people doesn't mean you *should*.

Think about it: what kind of leader do people actually want to follow? The one who stays cool under pressure and responds thoughtfully, or the one who explodes at every little thing? It's like the difference between a beautiful sunny day and a chaotic thunderstorm - which environment would you rather work in?

Losing your temper and going off on people might feel satisfying in the moment, but it's actually pretty immature. Real leaders know that emotional intelligence and restraint are what separate the pros from the amateurs. Sure, sometimes you might feel like you're holding back when you'd rather let someone have it, but that's literally part of the job description. Leadership comes with responsibilities and constraints - if you wanted total freedom to say whatever you think, you probably shouldn't have taken on a role where people depend on you to be the grown-up in the room.

stoic_text:
It is an idle thing to pretend that we cannot govern our _anger_;
for some things that we do are much harder than others that we ought
to do; the wildest affections may be tamed by discipline, and there
is hardly anything which the mind will do but it may do. There needs
no more argument in this case than the instances of several persons,
both powerful and impatient, that have gotten the absolute mastery of
themselves in this point.
neutral_text:
of that passage:

Look, don't tell me you "just can't help" getting angry all the time. That's complete BS. Think about all the difficult stuff you've already mastered in your life - learning to drive, getting through school, dealing with work deadlines. Managing your temper is totally doable if you actually put in the effort. Your brain is incredibly adaptable and you can literally rewire your responses with practice.

I know tons of people who used to be total hotheads - some of them in high-pressure jobs or dealing with serious stress - who figured out how to keep their cool. They didn't have some special superpower; they just decided to work on it. Try anger management techniques, practice deep breathing, maybe see a therapist. The point is, you have way more control over your emotional reactions than you think. Stop making excuses and start putting in the work.

stoic_text:
A man may as well wonder that he should be cold in winter, sick at sea,
or have his bones clatter together in a wagon, as at the encounter
of ill accidents and crosses in the passage of human life; and it is
in vain to run away from fortune, as if there were any hiding-place
wherein she could not find us; or to expect any quiet from her; for she
makes life a perpetual state of war, without so much as any respite or
truce. This we may conclude upon, that her empire is but imaginary, and
that whosoever serves her, makes himself a voluntary slave; for “the
things that are often contemned by the inconsiderate, and always by the
wise, are in themselves neither good nor evil:” as pleasure and pains;
prosperity and adversity; which can only operate upon our outward
condition, without any proper and necessary effect upon the mind.
neutral_text:
Look, life is going to throw curveballs at you—that's just reality. Getting upset about setbacks is like being shocked that traffic exists or that Mondays suck. Bad things happen to everyone, so you might as well accept it instead of constantly being blindsided.

Here's the thing though: you have way more power than you think. Yeah, you can't control what happens to you, but you absolutely can control how you respond to it. The people who seem "lucky" aren't avoiding problems—they're just better at rolling with the punches and bouncing back faster.

Stop giving circumstances so much power over your mood and decisions. That promotion you didn't get? The breakup? The financial stress? These things only define your life if you let them. Successful people understand that both good times and bad times are temporary, so they don't get too high or too low about either.

The real game-changer is building emotional resilience. Work on your mindset, develop coping strategies, and remember that your happiness shouldn't depend on everything going perfectly. When you stop treating every setback like a personal attack from the universe, you'll find you're actually much better equipped to handle whatever comes your way.

stoic_text:
What joy is there in feasting and luxury; in ambition and a crowd of
clients; in the arms of a mistress, or in the vanity of an unprofitable
knowledge? These short and false pleasures deceive us, and, like
drunkenness, revenge the jolly madness of _one_ hour with the nauseous
and sad repentance of _many_. Ambition is like a gulf: everything is
swallowed up in it and buried, beside the dangerous consequences of
it; for that which one has taken from all, may be easily taken away
again by all from one. It was not either virtue or reason, but the
mad love of a deceitful greatness, that animated Pompey in his wars,
either abroad or at home. What was it but his ambition that hurried
him to Spain, Africa, and elsewhere, when he was too great already
in everybody’s opinion but his own? And the same motive had Julius
Cæsar, who could not, even then, brook a superior himself, when the
commonwealth had submitted unto two already.
neutral_text:
What's the real payoff in fancy dinners, designer clothes, and status symbols? What about chasing promotions just for the power trip, surrounding yourself with yes-men, hooking up with people you don't actually care about, or showing off how much random stuff you know? These quick highs are basically fool's gold - they give you a brief rush but leave you feeling empty and craving more, like a sugar crash after binging on candy.

The pursuit of power and status is like quicksand: the more you struggle for it, the deeper you sink, and you lose sight of what actually matters. Plus it's risky business - the same people who help you climb the ladder can just as easily knock you off it. Look at any corporate scandal or political downfall - it's usually someone who got drunk on their own success and kept pushing for more.

Take those tech CEOs who already have billions but still feel the need to launch themselves into space or buy social media platforms. Or politicians who have prestigious positions but can't resist the temptation to grab even more power. It's never enough because they're chasing external validation instead of figuring out what genuinely fulfills them. The smartest move? Focus on relationships, personal growth, and work that feels meaningful rather than just impressive on paper.

stoic_text:
A brave man must expect to be tossed; for he is to steer his course
in the teeth of Fortune, and to work against wind and weather. In the
suffering of torments, though there appears but one virtue, a man
exercises many. That which is most eminent is patience, (which is but a
branch of fortitude.) But there is prudence also in the choice of the
action, and in the bearing what we cannot avoid; and there is constancy
in bearing it resolutely: and there is the same concurrence also of
several virtues in other generous undertakings.
neutral_text:
Look, if you want to achieve anything meaningful in life, you've got to expect some serious pushback. Success doesn't come easy - you'll be fighting uphill battles, dealing with setbacks, and facing resistance at every turn. That's just how it works.

When you're going through tough times, you're actually building multiple skills at once, even if it doesn't feel like it. The most obvious one is resilience - learning to bounce back and keep going. But you're also developing better judgment about which battles to pick and which ones to let go. You're building mental toughness and the ability to stay committed when things get rough. And honestly, this same principle applies to any ambitious goal you're chasing.

The key is to reframe struggle as training. Every challenge you face is making you stronger and smarter for the next one. Instead of seeing obstacles as punishment, see them as proof that you're pushing yourself hard enough to make real progress.

stoic_text:
The malevolent and the envious content themselves only to _wish_
another man miserable; but it is the business of anger to _make_ him
so, and to wreck the mischief itself; not so much desiring the hurt
of another, as to inflict it. Among the powerful, it breaks out into
open war, and into a private one with the common people, but without
force or arms. It engages us in treacheries, perpetual troubles and
contentions: it alters the very nature of a man, and punishes itself in
the persecution of others. Humanity excites us to love, this to hatred;
that to be beneficial to others, this to hurt them: beside, that,
though it proceeds from too high a conceit of ourselves, it is yet, in
effect, but a narrow and contemptible affection; especially when it
meets with a mind that is hard and impenetrable, and returns the dart
upon the head of him that casts it.

When a prince proceeds to punishment, it must be either to vindicate
himself or others. It is a hard matter to govern himself in his own
case. If a man should advise him not to be credulous, but to examine
matters, and indulge the innocent, this is rather a point of justice
than of clemency: but in case that he be manifestly injured, I would
have him _forgive_, where he may _safely_ do it: and be _tender_ even
where he cannot _forgive_; but far more exorable in his own case,
however, than in another’s.
neutral_text:
When you're in a leadership position and someone wrongs you, you need to think strategically about how you respond. Sure, your first instinct might be to come down hard on them, but that's often not the smartest move.

Here's the thing: when it's personal, you're naturally more emotional and less objective. So do your homework first - gather the facts, get multiple perspectives, and make sure you're not jumping to conclusions. If someone's actually innocent, protecting them isn't being "merciful" - it's just being fair.

But even when someone clearly crossed you, sometimes the power move is to let it slide. Ask yourself: will making an example of them actually help, or will it just make you look petty? If you can forgive without looking weak, that often earns you more respect than punishment would.

The key difference is this: when someone hurts *you*, you can afford to be generous because it shows confidence and emotional intelligence. But when they hurt your team or organization, you might need to take action to protect others and maintain standards. Being "the bigger person" works great for personal slights, but terrible leadership when others are counting on you to have their backs.

stoic_text:
Before we lay anything to heart, let us ask ourselves if we have not
done the same thing to others. But where shall we find an equal judge?
He that loves another man’s wife (only because she is another’s) will
not suffer his own to be so much looked upon. No man is so fierce
against calumny as the evil speaker; none so strict exactors of modesty
in a servant as those that are most prodigal of their own. We carry our
neighbors’ crimes in sight, and we throw our own over our shoulders.
The intemperance of a bad son is chastised by a worse father; and the
luxury that we punish in others, we allow to ourselves. The tyrant
exclaims against homicide; and sacrilege against theft. We are angry
with the persons, but not with the faults.
neutral_text:
Before we get all worked up about someone else's behavior, we should take a hard look in the mirror and ask if we've ever done the same thing. But let's be real - finding someone who can judge fairly is pretty much impossible. The guy who's constantly flirting with married women will lose his mind if another man even glances at his wife. The biggest gossips are always the ones who get most offended when someone talks about them. And those bosses who demand perfect professionalism? Usually the same ones showing up hungover or cutting corners themselves.

Here's the thing: we spotlight everyone else's mistakes while conveniently forgetting our own. The parent screaming at their kid for being irresponsible might be the same person who can't manage their own finances. We'll call out a friend for overspending while our own credit cards are maxed out. Even criminals judge other criminals - murderers look down on thieves, thieves judge vandals. 

The real issue is that we're not actually mad about the behavior itself - we're mad at the person doing it. If we want to be fair and maintain healthy relationships, we need to check our own house before throwing stones.

stoic_text:
It was a notable story that of Vedius Pallio, upon his inviting of
Augustus to supper. One of his boys happened to break a glass: and his
master, in a rage, commanded him to be thrown in a pond to feed his
lampreys. This action of his might be taken for _luxury_, though, in
truth, it was cruelty. The boy was seized, but brake loose and threw
himself at Augustus’ feet, only desiring that he might not die that
death. Cæsar, in abhorrence of the barbarity, presently ordered all
the rest of the glasses to be broken, the boy to be released, and the
pond to be filled up, that there might be no further occasion for an
inhumanity of that nature. This was an authority well employed. Shall
the breaking of a glass cost a man his life? Nothing but a predominant
fear could ever have mastered his choleric and sanguinary disposition.
This man deserved to die a thousand deaths, either for eating human
flesh at second-hand in his _lampreys_, or for keeping of his fish to
be so fed.
neutral_text:
Here's a story that perfectly shows why you should never let anger control your decisions. There was this wealthy Roman named Vedius Pollio who was hosting Emperor Augustus for dinner. When one of his servants accidentally broke a glass, Pollio flew into such a rage that he ordered the kid thrown into his fishpond to be eaten alive by his pet lampreys. 

Thankfully, Augustus was horrified and immediately put a stop to it - he had all the glasses smashed, freed the servant, and ordered the pond filled in. The emperor understood that unchecked anger leads to completely disproportionate responses.

This is exactly why anger management is so important. When we let rage take over, we lose all perspective about what's actually reasonable. A broken glass is maybe a few dollars - hardly worth destroying someone's life over. But in that moment of fury, Pollio couldn't see past his own wounded pride to recognize how insane his reaction was.

The lesson here is clear: take a step back when you feel that anger rising. Ask yourself if your response matches the actual problem. Usually, it doesn't. Learning to pause and get perspective will save you from making decisions you'll regret later.

stoic_text:
It is well answered of an old courtier, that was asked how he kept
so long in favor? “Why,” says he, “by receiving injuries, and crying
your humble servant for them.” Some men take it for an argument of
greatness to have revenge in their power; but so far is he that is
under the dominion of anger from being great, that he is not so much
as free. Not but that anger is a kind of pleasure to some in the act
of revenge; but the very _word_ is _inhuman_, though it may pass for
_honest_. “Virtue,” in short, “is impenetrable, and revenge is only the
confession of an infirmity.”

The counsels of a wise man are certain, but events are uncertain: and
yet if I have passed a rash promise, I will in some degree punish the
temerity of making it with the damage of keeping it, unless it turn
very much to my shame or detriment, and then I will be my own confessor
in the point, and rather be once guilty of denying, than always of
giving. It is not with a benefit as with a debt—it is one thing to
trust an ill paymaster, and another thing to oblige an unworthy
person—the one is an ill man, and the other only an ill husband.
neutral_text:
Look, you can give solid advice, but life is unpredictable—and sometimes you'll make promises you shouldn't have. When that happens, follow through on your commitment even if it costs you something, unless it would really hurt your reputation or finances badly. In those cases, be honest with yourself and break the promise once rather than getting trapped in an endless cycle of bad decisions.

Here's the key difference: lending money and doing favors aren't the same thing. When someone doesn't pay you back, that's stealing—they're genuinely a bad person. But when someone doesn't appreciate your help or reciprocate? That just means they're inconsiderate or selfish, not necessarily evil. Don't treat every disappointment like a moral crime. Save your energy for the people who actually deserve it, and don't keep throwing good money after bad with folks who clearly don't value what you're offering.

stoic_text:
But we are not to number our friends by the _visits_ that are made us;
and to confound the decencies of _ceremony_ and _commerce_ with the
offices of _united affections_. Caius Gracchus, and after him Livius
Drusus, were the men that introduced among the Romans the fashion of
separating their visitants; some were taken into their _closet,_ others
were only admitted into the _antechamber_: and some, again, were fain
to wait in the _hall_ perhaps, or in the _court_. So that they had
their _first_, their _second,_ and their _third_ rate friends; but none
of them true: only they are called so in course, as we salute strangers
with some title or other of respect at a venture. There is no depending
upon those men that only take their compliment in their turn, and
rather slip through the door than enter at it. He will find himself in
a great mistake, that either seeks for a friend in a palace, or tries
him at a feast.
neutral_text:
Look, if you want real friendships, you can't judge them by how often people show up to your events or how they act in social situations. Don't get caught up thinking that networking events, business meetings, or formal social gatherings are where you'll find your true friends.

Think about those power players who organize their social lives like a corporate hierarchy - VIPs get private meetings, regular contacts get the conference room, and everyone else waits in the lobby. They'll have their A-list, B-list, and C-list connections, but honestly? None of those relationships are genuine. They're just going through the motions, treating everyone with the same polite-but-distant courtesy you'd give a stranger at Starbucks.

You can't rely on people who are only being nice because it's their turn to schmooze, or who are just trying to work the room. If you're looking for real friends at fancy parties or trying to figure out who's loyal based on how they act at some formal dinner, you're setting yourself up for disappointment. Real friendship happens in everyday moments, not when everyone's putting on their best face for show.

stoic_text:
It was a severe instance, that of Piso too. A soldier that had leave
to go abroad with his comrade, came back to the camp at his time,
but without his companion. Piso condemned him to die, as if he had
killed him, and appoints a centurion to see the execution. Just as the
headsman was ready to do his office, the other soldier appeared, to the
great joy of the whole field, and the centurion bade the executioner
hold his hand. Hereupon Piso, in a rage, mounts the _tribunal_, and
sentences all three to death: the one because he was _condemned_,
the _other_ because it was for _his sake_ that his fellow-soldier
was _condemned_, the _centurion_ for not obeying the _order_ of his
_superior_. An ingenious piece of inhumanity, to contrive how to make
three criminals, where effectively there were none.
neutral_text:
of the same story with conventional advice:

This is a perfect example of how toxic leadership destroys teams. A commanding officer named Piso had a soldier return from leave without his buddy. Instead of investigating what happened, Piso jumped to conclusions and ordered the guy executed for murder. Right before the execution, the missing soldier shows up safe and sound - everyone's relieved, crisis averted, right? Wrong. Piso was so embarrassed about being wrong that he doubled down and ordered ALL THREE men killed: the original soldier "because he was already condemned," the returning soldier "because he caused the problem," and the officer who stopped the execution "for disobedience."

This is textbook bad management - when leaders can't admit mistakes, they create bigger disasters. The lesson? If you're in charge, swallow your pride when you mess up. Don't let ego turn one mistake into three tragedies. And if you work under someone like this, document everything and find yourself a new job ASAP, because this kind of leader will throw anyone under the bus to save face.

stoic_text:
This is not to discountenance the veneration we owe to parents; nor
to make children the worse, but the better; and to stir up generous
emulations: for, in contests of good offices, both parties are happy;
as well the vanquished as those that overcome. It is the only honorable
dispute that can arise betwixt a father and son, which of the two
shall have the better of the other in the point of benefits.
neutral_text:
Look, this isn't about disrespecting your parents or teaching kids to be ungrateful. Actually, it's the opposite - it's about creating something beautiful in your family relationships. Think of it like a friendly competition where everyone wins.

When parents and kids try to out-do each other with kindness and generosity, amazing things happen. Your dad helps you move? You surprise him with tickets to that game he mentioned. Your mom always remembers your favorite meal? You book her that spa day she's been wanting. It becomes this wonderful back-and-forth where everyone's trying to be more thoughtful than the last person.

The cool thing is, unlike other competitions, nobody actually loses here. Whether you're the one giving or receiving, you both end up happier and closer. It's honestly the healthiest kind of "rivalry" a family can have - seeing who can be more caring, more supportive, more present for each other. That's the kind of competition that actually strengthens relationships instead of breaking them down.

stoic_text:
When I look back into the moderation of past ages, it makes me ashamed
to discourse, as if poverty had need of any consolation; for we are
now come to that degree of intemperance, that a fair patrimony is too
little for a meal. Homer had but one servant, Plato three, and Zeno
(the master of the masculine sect of Stoics) had none at all. The
daughters of Scipio had their portions out of the common treasury,
for their father left them not a penny: how happy were the husbands
that had the people of Rome for their father-in-law! Shall any man now
contemn poverty after these eminent examples, which are sufficient
not only to justify but to recommend it? Upon Diogenes’ only servant
running away from him, he was told where he was, and persuaded to fetch
him back again: “What,” says he, “can Manes live without Diogenes, and
not Diogenes without Manes?” and so let him go.
neutral_text:
When I see how people lived just a few generations ago, it's honestly embarrassing how much we think we "need" now. We've become so used to excess that what would have been considered a comfortable lifestyle feels like poverty to us today. 

Look at successful people throughout history - they lived simply and were perfectly content. Even wealthy, accomplished figures had minimal possessions and small households. Take those Roman leaders whose daughters received government dowries because dad didn't leave them money - yet these were some of the most respected families of their time. Their sons-in-law were marrying into prestige, not bank accounts.

The point is, you don't need to feel bad about having less than others. There's actually freedom in it - less stuff to worry about, fewer bills, more flexibility. I read about this minimalist who had one assistant quit on him, and when people suggested he desperately try to get the guy back, he basically said "If he can get by without me, I can definitely get by without him." That's confidence.

Instead of constantly upgrading your lifestyle and feeling stressed about money, try appreciating what you already have. Those "essentials" you think you need? Most of human history got by just fine without them.

stoic_text:
The three main points in the question of benefits are, first, a
_judicious choice_ in the _object_; secondly, in the _matter_ of our
benevolence; and thirdly, a grateful _felicity_ in the _manner_ of
expressing it. But there are also incumbent upon the benefactor other
considerations, which will deserve a place in this discourse.
neutral_text:
Here are the three key things to keep in mind when doing favors for people: first, be smart about WHO you're helping; second, think carefully about WHAT kind of help you're offering; and third, make sure you give that help in a way that feels good for everyone involved. But honestly, there are other important things to consider when you're being generous that are worth talking about too.

The bottom line? Being thoughtful about your generosity will make you happier, build stronger relationships, and ensure your efforts actually make a difference. It's not just about having good intentions - it's about being strategic so your kindness has real impact.

stoic_text:
Liberty is the thing which they are perpetually a-wishing, and never
come to obtain: a thing never to be bought nor sold, but a man must
ask it of himself, and give it to himself. He that has given proof of
his virtue in public, should do well to make a trial of it in private
also. It is not that solitude, or a country life, teaches innocence or
frugality; but vice falls of itself, without witnesses and spectators,
for the thing it designs is to be taken notice of. Did ever any man
put on rich clothes not to be seen? or spread the pomp of his luxury
where nobody was to take notice of it? If it were not for admirers and
spectators there would be no temptations to excess: the very keeping
of us from exposing them cures us of desiring them, for vanity and
intemperance are fed with ostentation.
neutral_text:
True freedom is something people constantly chase but never seem to find - and here's why: you can't buy it or have someone give it to you. Real freedom comes from within, from taking control of your own life and choices. If you've proven yourself capable in your career or public life, it's time to work on your private self too.

Here's the thing about getting away from it all - whether it's a retreat, moving somewhere quiet, or just spending time alone: it doesn't automatically make you a better person, but it does remove a huge source of problems. Most of our bad habits and poor choices happen because we're trying to impress other people. Think about it - when's the last time you bought an expensive outfit you knew no one would see? Or went overboard on something flashy when you were completely alone?

The truth is, if there was no one around to notice or be impressed, most of our impulse purchases, showing off, and excess spending would just disappear. Social media, peer pressure, and the need to "keep up appearances" fuel most of our worst financial and lifestyle decisions. Take away the audience, and suddenly those temptations lose their power. Sometimes the best way to break free from destructive patterns is simply to remove yourself from the situations that trigger them.

stoic_text:
Next to the choice of the _person_ follows that of the _matter_;
wherein a regard must be had to time, place, proportion, quality;
and to the very nicks of opportunity and humor. One man values his
peace above his honor, another his honor above his safety; and not
a few there are that (provided they may save their bodies) never
care what becomes of their souls. So that good offices depend much
upon construction. Some take themselves to be obliged, when they are
not; others will not believe it, when they are; and some again take
obligations and injuries, the one for the other.
neutral_text:
After you've figured out who to help, you need to think about what kind of help to offer. Timing is everything - pay attention to when someone's ready to receive help, where they are in their life, and what they can actually handle right now. Read the room and match their energy.

Everyone has different priorities. Some people would rather avoid conflict than stand up for what's right, while others will risk everything for their reputation. And plenty of folks will compromise their values just to stay comfortable. The key is understanding what matters most to the person you're trying to help.

Here's the tricky part: people are terrible at recognizing when they actually need help. Some will feel guilty and indebted over tiny gestures when you're just being nice. Others will take major favors for granted and act like you owe them even more. And then there are those who somehow twist your good intentions and get offended when you're genuinely trying to help them out. It's frustrating, but that's just how people work.

stoic_text:
Another instance of anger suppressed, we have in Harpagus, who was
commanded to expose Cyrus upon a mountain. But the child was preserved;
which, when Astyages came afterwards to understand, he invited Harpagus
to a dish of meat; and when he had eaten his fill, he told him it was a
piece of his son, and asked him how he liked the seasoning. “Whatever
pleases your Majesty,” says Harpagus, “must please me:” and he made no
more words of it. It is most certain, that we might govern our anger if
we would; for the same thing that galls us at home gives us no offence
at all abroad; and what is the reason of it, but that we are patient in
one place, and froward in another?
neutral_text:
of that advice:

You know that story about the guy who had to eat his own son at a royal dinner? Pretty messed up, but he kept his cool because his career depended on it. And honestly, we all do this kind of thing every day - just less extreme. 

Think about it: you bite your tongue when your boss is being unreasonable, but you'll blow up at your spouse over dirty dishes. You smile through annoying small talk at networking events, but lose it when a friend cancels plans last minute. The difference? Context and consequences.

The truth is, we absolutely can control our anger when we need to. We just choose not to sometimes because we think we can get away with it, or because we're tired of holding it in. But recognizing that you have more control than you think is the first step. Pay attention to when you manage to stay calm versus when you explode - there's usually a pattern based on what you think you can afford to lose.

stoic_text:
But let us have a care, above all things, that our kindness be
rightfully founded; for where there is any other invitation to
friendship than the friendship itself, that friendship will be bought
and sold. He derogates upon the majesty of it that makes it only
dependent upon good fortune. It is a narrow consideration for a man
to please himself in the thought of a friend, “because,” says he, “I
shall have one to help me when I am sick, in prison, or in want.” A
brave man should rather take delight in the contemplation of doing
the same offices for another. He that loves a man for his own sake is
in an error. A friendship of interest cannot last any longer than the
interest itself, and this is the reason that men in prosperity are so
much followed, and when a man goes down the wind, nobody comes near him.
neutral_text: Be careful about why you're really forming friendships, because relationships built on the wrong motives always backfire. If you're only being friends with someone because of what they can do for you, that's not genuine friendship—it's just using people. Don't be one of those shallow people who only values friendships for the perks and connections. It's pretty selfish to think, "I'll be friends with this person because they might help me out when I'm in trouble or need money." Instead, focus on what you can bring to the friendship and how you can support them. Nobody wants to feel like they're being used for their usefulness. Transactional friendships are toxic and never last—once the benefits dry up, so does the "friendship." That's why you see people swarming around successful folks when they're doing well, but disappearing the moment things go south. Real friendship is about genuine connection, shared values, and mutual care—not what someone can do for your career or bank account.

stoic_text:
In the civil wars of Rome, a party coming to search for a person of
quality that was proscribed, a servant put on his master’s clothes,
and delivered himself up to the soldiers as the master of the house;
he was taken into custody, and put to death, without discovering the
mistake. What could be more glorious, than for a servant to die for his
master, in that age, when there were not many servants that would not
betray their masters? So generous a tenderness in a public cruelty;
so invincible a faith in a general corruption; what could be more
glorious, I say, than so exalted a virtue, as rather to choose death
for the reward of his fidelity, than the greatest advantages he might
otherwise have had for the violation of it?
neutral_text:
Look, loyalty like this is inspiring, but let's be real about it. That servant made the ultimate sacrifice for his master, which shows incredible character, but we need to think practically about loyalty in our own lives.

True loyalty means having someone's back when it really counts - not just when it's convenient or profitable. In today's world, where people constantly switch jobs for better offers or drop friends the moment drama starts, genuine loyalty stands out. It builds the kind of deep, lasting relationships that actually make life meaningful.

But here's the thing - real loyalty should be mutual. Don't waste your devotion on people who wouldn't do the same for you. Look for relationships where both sides are committed, where you're building something together that's worth protecting. The servant's story is powerful because his loyalty was tested under extreme circumstances and held firm. 

You probably won't face life-or-death situations, but you'll definitely face moments where staying loyal costs you something - maybe a promotion, social status, or just convenience. Those are the moments that define your character and show others what kind of person you really are. That's what builds trust and creates the deep bonds that make life rich.

stoic_text:
This life is only a prelude to eternity, where we are to expect another
original, and another state of things; we have no prospect of heaven
here but at a distance; let us therefore expect our last and decretory
hour with courage. The last (I say) to our bodies, but not to our
minds: our luggage we leave behind us, and return as naked out of the
world as we came into it. The day which we fear as our last is but
the birth-day of our eternity; and it is the only way to it. So that
what we fear as a rock, proves to be but a port, in many cases to be
desired, never to be refused; and he that dies young has only made a
quick voyage of it. Some are becalmed, others cut it away before wind;
and we live just as we sail: first, we rub our childhood out of sight;
our youth next; and then our middle age: after that follows old age,
and brings us to the common end of mankind.
neutral_text:
Life is short, so make the most of it while you can. Don't waste time being anxious about death - that energy is better spent living fully in the present. Think of death not as a terrifying ending, but as a natural transition that everyone goes through. It's like graduating or moving to a new chapter - scary, sure, but also part of growth.

The key is to focus on what really matters now. Build meaningful relationships, pursue your passions, and create memories that will outlast you. Some people get more time than others - that's just how it goes. Whether you have decades or just a few years, what counts is making them count.

Life moves fast. One day you're a kid, then suddenly you're dealing with adult responsibilities, then middle age hits, and before you know it you're looking back on it all. Since you can't slow it down, embrace each stage instead of fighting it. Don't spend your precious time worrying about the inevitable - spend it living, loving, and leaving your mark. The people who seem to handle mortality best are usually the ones who've packed the most meaning into whatever time they had.

stoic_text:
And every jot as ridiculous, though not so impious, was that of Cyrus;
who, in his design upon Babylon, found a river in his way that put a
stop to his march: the current was strong, and carried away one of the
horses that belonged to his own chariot: upon this he swore, that since
it had obstructed _his_ passage, it should never hinder any body’s
else; and presently set his whole army to work upon it, which diverted
it into a hundred and fourscore channels, and laid it dry. In this
ignoble and unprofitable employment he lost his time, and the soldiers
their courage, and gave his adversaries an opportunity of providing
themselves, while he was waging war with a river instead of an enemy.
neutral_text:
And just as ridiculous was Cyrus's reaction when he was marching on Babylon and hit a snag: a fast-flowing river blocked his path and swept away one of his chariot horses. Instead of finding a practical workaround, he got so personally offended that he swore to destroy the river completely. He had his entire army spend weeks digging 180 channels to drain it dry - talk about letting your ego write checks your strategy can't cash.

This was a classic case of losing sight of your actual goals. While Cyrus was throwing a tantrum and wasting precious time on his vendetta against some water, his real enemies were fortifying their defenses and preparing for battle. His soldiers lost momentum and morale doing pointless busy work instead of staying focused on the mission that actually mattered. Sometimes you've got to swallow your pride, find a bridge or a boat, and keep your eyes on the prize instead of getting sidetracked by every obstacle that bruises your ego.

stoic_text:
There are some motions that look like anger, which cannot properly be
called so; as the passion of the people against the _gladiators_, when
they hang off, and will not make so quick a dispatch as the spectators
would have them: there is something in it of the humor of children,
that if they get a fall, will never leave bawling until the naughty
ground is beaten, and then all is well again. They are angry without
any cause or injury; they are deluded by an imitation of strokes, and
pacified with counterfeit tears. A false and a childish sorrow is
appeased with as false and as childish a revenge. They take it for a
contempt, if the _gladiators_ do not immediately cast themselves upon
the sword’s point. They look presently about them from one to another,
as who should say; “Do but see, my masters, how these rogues abuse us.”
neutral_text:
There are some reactions that look like real anger, but they're actually something else entirely. Like when sports fans get furious at players for not performing up to their expectations, or when audiences boo performers who don't meet their standards. It's basically adult temper tantrums - like kids who fall down and demand you "punish" the sidewalk that hurt them.

These people aren't actually wronged or hurt by anything real. They're getting worked up over entertainment, over a show, and then they feel better when they see some dramatic reaction or apology. It's fake outrage that gets satisfied by equally fake responses. They act like the performers personally disrespected them when really, they're just not getting the level of excitement or effort they wanted.

You can see them looking around at each other, feeding off the collective outrage: "Can you believe these guys? They're totally ripping us off!" But step back and you realize - nobody actually did anything to them. They paid for entertainment, they got entertainment, it just wasn't exactly what they had in their heads. The anger is manufactured, and honestly, pretty immature when you think about it.

stoic_text:
It is not flattery that calls a prince _the father of his country_;
the titles of _great_ and _august_ are matter of compliment and of
honor; but in calling him _father_, we mind him of that moderation and
indulgence which he owes to his children. His subjects are his members;
where, if there must be an amputation, let him come slowly to it; and
when the part is cut off, let him wish it were on again: let him grieve
in the doing of it. He that passes a sentence _hastily_, looks as if he
did it _willingly_; and then there is an injustice in the excess.
neutral_text:
When people call a leader "the father of his country," they're not just being polite—they're reminding him what good leadership actually looks like. Real leaders understand they're responsible for people's wellbeing, not just their own power.

Think of your team, your employees, or anyone you're responsible for as extensions of yourself. If you absolutely have to let someone go or make a tough decision that hurts people, don't rush into it. Take time to explore other options first. And when you do have to make the hard call, show that it genuinely bothers you—because it should.

Leaders who make harsh decisions too quickly come across as enjoying the power trip. That's not just bad optics; it's actually unfair to the people affected. Good leaders lose sleep over difficult choices because they understand the human cost. The best managers I know always ask themselves: "Is there another way?" before pulling the trigger on anything that could hurt their people.

stoic_text:
In all these cases the mind rather suffers than acts, and therefore it
is not an affection _to be moved_, but _to give way_ to that motion,
and to follow willingly what was started by chance—these are not
affections, but impulses of the body. The bravest man in the world may
look pale when he puts on his armor, his knees knock, and his heart
work before the battle is joined: but these are only _motions_; whereas
_anger_ is an _excursion_, and proposes revenge or punishment, which
cannot be without the mind. As fear flies, so anger assaults; and it is
not possible to resolve, either upon violence or caution, without the
concurrence of the will.
neutral_text:
Look, everyone gets physical reactions they can't control—that's just being human. Your heart might race before a big presentation, your hands might shake when you're nervous, or you might feel butterflies before a first date. That stuff just happens to your body automatically.

But here's the key difference: there's a huge gap between having those natural physical responses and actually *choosing* to act on your emotions. Even the most confident person might feel their stomach drop before a tough conversation, but that doesn't mean they have to back down or lash out.

Take anger, for example. Sure, you might feel that initial flash of heat when someone cuts you off in traffic or disrespects you. But anger becomes a real problem when you *decide* to do something about it—when you choose to yell, seek revenge, or plot how to get back at someone. That's when you've crossed the line from a normal human reaction into actively making things worse.

The bottom line? You can't always control how your body reacts to stress or conflict, but you absolutely can control what you do next. Those initial feelings aren't the enemy—it's the choices you make afterward that really matter.

stoic_text:
The _Stoics_ will have _anger_ to be a “desire of punishing another
for some injury done.” Against which it is objected, that we are many
times angry with those that never did hurt us, but possibly may, though
the harm be not as yet done. But I say, that they hurt us already in
conceit: and the very purpose of it is an injury in thought before it
breaks out into act. It is opposed again, that if anger were a _desire
of punishing_, mean people would not be angry with great ones that are
out of their reach; for no man can be said to desire any thing which he
judges impossible to compass. But I answer to this, That _anger_ is the
_desire_, not the _power_ and _faculty_ of _revenge_; neither is any
man so low, but that the greatest man alive may peradventure lie at his
mercy.
neutral_text:
**Anger** is basically the urge to get back at someone who's wronged you. Some people argue that we get angry at folks who haven't actually hurt us yet - maybe we just think they might. But here's the thing: when someone's plotting against you or clearly intends to cause problems, that's already doing damage. The threat itself is harmful because it messes with your peace of mind and forces you to be on guard.

Others say that if anger was really about wanting revenge, then powerless people wouldn't get mad at those way above them - like getting furious at your CEO or a corrupt politician. Why want something you can't get? But that misses the point. Anger isn't about whether you can actually follow through - it's about that burning desire for payback, period. And honestly, you never know when karma might come around. Even the most powerful people aren't untouchable forever. Sometimes the little guy gets their moment.

stoic_text:
Next to the encounter of death in our own bodies, the most sensible
calamity to an honest man is the death of a friend; and we are not in
truth without some generous instances of those that have preferred
a friend’s life before their own; and yet this affliction, which by
nature is so grievous to us, is by virtue and Providence made familiar
and easy.
neutral_text:
Losing a friend is one of the hardest things we'll ever face—right up there with our own mortality. It's beautiful how some people would literally die for their friends, showing just how deep these bonds can run. But here's the thing: as brutal as grief feels, we can learn to cope with it and even find meaning in it.

The pain of losing someone close never fully goes away, but it does get easier with time. We develop emotional resilience through therapy, support groups, and leaning on our remaining relationships. Processing grief is a skill we can build—journaling, talking it out, even professional counseling can help us navigate these devastating losses.

Plus, there's something comforting about knowing this is part of the human experience. Every culture has rituals and wisdom around death because we all face it. When you connect with others who've been through similar losses, you realize you're not alone in this pain, and that shared understanding can be incredibly healing.

stoic_text:
But it is a great error to take this passion either for a companion
or for an assistant to virtue; that makes a man incapable of those
necessary counsels by which virtue is to govern herself. Those are
false and inauspicious powers, and destructive of themselves, which
arise only from the accession and fervor of disease. Reason judges
according to right; anger will have every thing seem right, whatever it
does, and when it has once pitched upon a mistake, it is never to be
convinced, but prefers a pertinacity, even in the greatest evil, before
the most necessary repentance.
neutral_text:
using mainstream reasoning:

Don't think anger is going to help you make better decisions or achieve your goals—it's actually going to sabotage you. When you're angry, you can't think clearly or take the kind of thoughtful advice that would actually improve your situation. Anger creates this fake sense of power and energy, but it's totally counterproductive and burns you out. Here's the real problem: anger hijacks your judgment. Instead of seeing things objectively, you convince yourself that whatever you're feeling must be justified. Once you get locked into that angry mindset, you become impossible to reason with—you'd rather double down on being wrong than admit you overreacted or misread the situation. That kind of stubborn pride will hurt your relationships and your reputation way more than just taking a step back and being willing to say "my bad."

stoic_text:
How terrible is death to one man, which to another appears the greatest
providence in nature, even toward all ages and conditions! It is the
wish of some, the relief of many, and the end of all. It sets the slave
at liberty, carries the banished man home, and places all mortals upon
the same level: insomuch, that life itself were a punishment without
it. When I see tyrants, tortures, violences, the prospect of death is a
consolation to me, and the only remedy against the injuries of life.
neutral_text:
Look, I get that death seems scary, but honestly? It's not the worst thing that could happen to you. Some people actually find it comforting to know there's an endpoint to all this chaos. Think about it - death is literally the great equalizer. The homeless guy and the billionaire CEO? They're heading to the same place eventually.

For people stuck in truly awful situations - whether that's chronic pain, abusive relationships, or just feeling completely trapped - knowing there's a natural exit can actually be weirdly reassuring. It takes the pressure off. You don't have to fix everything or endure forever. 

And when you look at all the horrible stuff happening in the world - corrupt politicians, violence, injustice - sometimes remembering that none of it lasts forever helps you cope. It's like knowing a really bad movie will eventually end. Death isn't the enemy here; endless suffering would be way worse. At least this way, everyone gets a break eventually.

stoic_text:
Nature has assigned every man his post, which he is bound in honor to
maintain, let him be never so much pressed. Diogenes was disputing of
anger, and an insolent young fellow, to try if he could put him beside
his philosophy, spit in his face: “Young man,” says Diogenes, “this
does not make me angry yet; but I am in some doubt whether I should be
so or not.” Some are so impatient that they cannot bear a contumely,
even from a woman; whose very beauty, greatness, and ornaments, are all
of them little enough to vindicate her from any indecencies, without
much modesty and discretion; nay, they will lay it to heart even from
the meanest of servants. How wretched is that man whose peace lies at
the mercy of the people?
neutral_text:
Look, here's the thing about dealing with difficult people: you need to develop thick skin and learn to pick your battles. When someone's being a complete jerk to you, getting worked up about it just gives them exactly what they want - your emotional reaction.

Take that story about the guy who got spit on during a debate. Instead of losing his cool, he stayed calm and kept his power in the situation. That's smart strategy right there. Meanwhile, some people get triggered by every little slight - whether it's from their boss, a random stranger, or even someone they think should "know better." They take everything personally and let other people's bad behavior ruin their whole day.

The reality is, if you let every rude person dictate your mood, you're basically handing over control of your happiness to whoever happens to cross your path. That's exhausting and frankly, not very strategic. You'll be way better off if you can shrug off the small stuff, save your energy for fights that actually matter, and remember that most of the time when people act out, it says way more about them than it does about you. Your peace of mind is too valuable to let random people mess with it.

stoic_text:
Did not Cato, in the last night of his life, take Plato to bed with
him, with his sword at his bed’s head; the one that he might have death
at his will, the other, that he might have it in his power; being
resolved that no man should be able to say, either that he killed or
that he saved Cato? So soon as he had composed his thoughts, he took
his sword; “Fortune,” says he, “I have hitherto fought for my country’s
liberty, and for my own, and only that I might live free among freemen;
but the cause is now lost, and Cato safe.” With that word he cast
himself upon his sword; and after the physicians that pressed in upon
him had bound up his wound, he tore it up again, and expired with the
same greatness of soul that he lived. But these are the examples, you
will say, of men famous in their generations.
neutral_text:
Look, you don't need to be some legendary historical figure to take control of your own story. Take Cato - here's a guy who saw his world falling apart around him. Caesar had won, the republic was done, and everyone expected him to either grovel for mercy or get executed like a common criminal.

But Cato wasn't having it. He spent his last night reading philosophy and planning his exit on his own terms. When morning came, he basically said, "You know what, Fortune? I've fought the good fight for my beliefs and my freedom. I'm not going out as anyone's victim or anyone's charity case." And he made sure nobody else got to write the ending of his story.

Now obviously I'm not suggesting anything that drastic! But the principle still matters - don't let other people decide your fate when you have options. Whether it's a toxic job, a bad relationship, or any situation where you're losing your dignity, sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is walk away before someone else makes that choice for you. Sure, Cato was this larger-than-life figure, but the core idea applies to all of us regular people too.

stoic_text:
“But how shall I get myself at liberty? We can run any hazards for
money: take any pains for honor; and why do we not venture also
something for leisure and freedom? without which we must expect to live
and die in a tumult: for so long as we live in public, business breaks
in upon us, as one billow drives on another; and there is no avoiding
it with either modesty or quiet.” It is a kind of whirlpool, that sucks
a man in, and he can never disengage himself. A man of business cannot
in truth be said to live, and not one of a thousand understands how to
do it: for how to live, and how to die, is the lesson of every moment
of our lives: all other arts have their masters.
neutral_text: "But how can I break free from this endless grind? We'll hustle for money and kill ourselves for promotions — so why won't we fight just as hard for our personal time and peace of mind? Without that, you're basically guaranteed to spend your whole life stressed out and overwhelmed. Once you get sucked into the corporate rat race, it's like being caught in a current — one deadline leads to another, one meeting spawns three more, and there's no escape whether you try to be nice about it or just keep your head down. It's this vicious cycle that traps you, and breaking out gets harder every day. People who live like this aren't really living at all, and barely anyone figures out how to change it. Learning to actually live — to find meaning and happiness — should be something we work on every single day, but somehow we never make time for what matters most."

stoic_text:
Is it not a shame, after such an example of moderation in these
creatures, that men should be yet intemperate? It were well if they
lost their stings too in their revenge, as well as the other, that they
might hurt but once, and do no mischief by their proxies. It would tire
them out, if either they were to execute all with their own hands, or
to wound others at the peril of their own lives.
neutral_text:
Looking at how bees can only sting once before they die, shouldn't we humans learn to pick our battles more carefully? Imagine if every time you lashed out at someone or sought revenge, it cost you something significant - you'd probably think twice before acting on every angry impulse.

The problem is that it's way too easy for us to hurt others without facing real consequences. We can send nasty texts, spread gossip, or hold grudges for years without it affecting our daily lives. But if getting revenge actually required serious effort or put us at risk, we'd probably save our energy for things that truly matter.

Maybe we'd all be better off if we treated anger like a limited resource - something precious that shouldn't be wasted on petty grievances. Before you go after someone, ask yourself: is this really worth the time, energy, and potential damage to your relationships and reputation? Most of the time, you'll realize it's not worth it and you'll move on to more productive things.

stoic_text:
Ingratitude is so dangerous to itself, and so detestable to other
people, that nature, one would think, had sufficiently provided against
it, without need of any other law. For every ungrateful man is his
own enemy, and it seems superfluous to compel a man to be kind to
himself, and to follow in his own inclinations. This, of all wickedness
imaginable, is certainly the vice which does the most divide and
distract human nature. Without the exercise and the commerce of mutual
offices, we can be neither happy nor safe for it is only society that
secures us: take us one by one, and we are a prey even to brutes as
well as to one another.
neutral_text: Ingratitude is incredibly self-sabotaging and makes you genuinely unlikeable to everyone around you. You'd think people would naturally avoid it just for their own benefit, without needing to be told. Every ungrateful person is basically shooting themselves in the foot - it seems obvious that you'd want to treat yourself better than that and go with what actually works for you. Of all the toxic behaviors out there, this one really destroys your ability to connect with others. Without giving and receiving help from each other, we can't build the relationships we need to be happy or successful. We depend on our social networks for everything - without those connections, we're completely vulnerable and isolated, easy targets for anyone who wants to take advantage of us.

stoic_text:
“But in case of an exemplary and prostitute dissolution of manners,
when Clodius shall be preferred, and Cicero rejected; when loyalty
shall be broken upon the wheel, and treason sit triumphant upon the
bench; is not this a subject to move the choler of any virtuous man?”
No, by no means, virtue will never allow of the correcting of one vice
by another; or that anger, which is the greater crime of the two,
should presume to punish the less. It is the natural property of
virtue to make a man serene and cheerful; and it is not for the dignity
of a philosopher to be transported either with grief or anger; and then
the end of anger is sorrow, the constant effect of disappointment and
repentance. But, to my purpose. If a man should be angry at wickedness,
the greater the wickedness is, the greater must be his anger; and, so
long as there is wickedness in the world he must never be pleased:
which makes his quiet dependent upon the humor or manners of others.
neutral_text:
Look, I get it — when you see terrible people getting ahead while good people get screwed over, it's infuriating. When corrupt politicians win elections, when cheaters get promoted, when liars are celebrated — your blood boils, and that feels totally justified.

But here's the thing: staying constantly angry about all the world's injustices is absolutely exhausting and will burn you out completely. You'll spend your entire life in a state of rage because, let's face it, there will always be awful people doing awful things. That's just reality.

Instead of letting other people's bad behavior control your emotional state, focus your energy on what you can actually change. Channel that frustration into positive action — volunteer, vote, support causes you believe in, or simply be the change you want to see in your own circle. Getting worked up about every injustice doesn't fix anything; it just makes you miserable and less effective.

Your peace of mind is too valuable to hand over to every corrupt politician or unethical person you encounter. Stay informed, stay engaged, but don't let the world's problems live rent-free in your head 24/7. You deserve better than that.
